The Development Committee adopted my report unanimously. Some of our points have been accepted, but on other points we are still rather dissatisfied.

I would like to express my thanks, because it is clear that we have made progress compared to last year: we have gone from 449 to 518 admissible cases, from 12% to 16% of the total, and we have also seen an increase in the number of investigations carried forward.

However, and this is my appeal, we still need to increase our efforts in terms of communication.
Let us study together effective methods, perhaps starting with our schools, to ensure that citizens receive quality information on the role and skills of the European Ombudsman.
There are interesting projects in this regard, launched this year, which need to be implemented and supported.
In this way, the already good figures of today will certainly continue to improve.

Mr President, I appreciate the great efforts made by our rapporteur, Mr Capoulas Santos, to bring the CAP closer to farmers and European citizens, rather than to market requirements, and to reconcile the various concerns in the face of the many challenges at stake.

It is therefore essential that the 27 Member States adopt a common approach to the sector, but it is also important to maintain the instruments for supporting and managing the markets for the main products.

I am thinking, for example, of the dairy sector or of aid for tobacco, the abolition of which would certainly not contribute to reducing smoking, but rather to the elimination of an important European production and the social fabric associated with it.
These instruments must, however, always take into account the extremely varied nature of the European scene.
The milk quota system currently in force, for example, is based on criteria that are now obsolete.

We therefore need a more flexible system that gives each Member State the opportunity to respond to its own needs, which would also boost the European competitiveness of the sector on the international market.

This also concerns the Blue Card: there is resistance to some central principles, such as equal pay for equal work, which is a sacrosanct and natural principle, but even on this we have encountered some sharpness.

We believe it is essential to overcome the principle of Community preference, to overcome the idea that there is a Europe travelling at two speeds, and therefore we need to make the principle of Community preference prevail.
We believe that the call to the labour market is important, but the labour market cannot be the only regulating principle.
There are other inclusive principles of political and social civilisation that must be included in immigration policies.
Legal immigration means equal dignity and opportunity, or shall we go back to selective, partial and discriminatory immigration?

For this reason, my group supports Mr Moreno ’ s proposal to the Commission: let us try to think of a Blue Card that is not limited to that 3% of highly qualified immigrants, but that seeks to find legal and concrete instruments to open up the market to immigration.
Migration must be inclusion; if it becomes selection, it is no longer a positive policy.

Honourable Mr. Mark, Honourable Toia, one minute. In the face of the very serious crisis affecting the car industry from America to our continent, Europe is faced with a very clear dilemma: either remain inert and watch this collapse of demand and

production, the effects of which cannot even be calculated today, in the name of an abstract consistency, for a theoretical model of market liberalism, of total respect for competition, as some voices have said today, or to take on its responsibilities to adequately face the situation?
We are definitely in favour of this second option, and we call on the Commission and the Council to make a decision and show determination.
We appreciated the words of Commissioner Füle and we hope that some of our colleagues will not dampen this determination too much.
This is an exceptional situation, so the responses must also be exceptional.

Moreover, ladies and gentlemen, the financial crisis has shown us that some political and economic authorities have made choices about the conduct of public investment and the use of public resources that were unthinkable a few months ago.

But our intervention must be selective: we must reconcile the strategy with the emergency, supporting, through both easier credit and demand support, a selective orientation towards environmentally more sustainable production.

However, such positions underestimate the future price of fossil fuels and ignore the considerable benefits of the rapid spread of renewable energy, such as security of supply and, above all, the creation of new jobs at a time of recession when thousands of workers are being laid off.

No, that is not the case. As a politician, I do not act on behalf of corporations or trade unions; I act on behalf of citizens.
I think of them when I vote, and I think of their social rights, which are fundamental to me in Europe.

I do not think, therefore, that a Europe that does not move forward while the world moves forward, and that instead makes glaring errors of perspective, confusing weakening with protection, protection with flexibility and freedom, is a good Europe.
This is all the more serious at a time when Europe is experiencing its worst crisis and sees no prospects for prosperity and growth.

If we do not understand, Mr President, representatives of the Council and of the Commission, if we do not understand that at this time millions of workers are at risk of losing their jobs and feel weak and insecure,

Mr President, we are facing an important compromise for Europe, even if the European Parliament has had to swallow some bitter pills.
On the substance, the text on renewables that emerged from the trialogue contains a clear definition of the objectives and, above all, of their binding nature.
The 2014 review clause should be interpreted as allowing greater flexibility in order to achieve the target of a 20% reduction, and, if the conditions are right, to push it up to 30% by 2020.

It should be stressed that the most powerful instrument of flexibility and the most economically advantageous for Member States lies in national measures for efficiency in the construction, transport and industry sectors and in better use of electricity.
The mandatory target of 10% for biofuels must be qualified by requirements for efficiency in the transformation processes, by the introduction of environmental and social sustainability criteria,
The use of biomass should be directed towards non-controversial areas and towards more efficient conversion technologies, as compared to first- and second-generation biofuels.
The agreement reached by the Council and Parliament ’ s willingness send a positive message: the very serious economic crisis cannot be tackled without a change of strategy.
Those who, like Berlusconi, made fun of the European directives on the 20-20-20 targets, calling them a Don Quixote plan, have been defeated.

The package that this Parliament is called upon to approve, despite the watering down due to the Member States ’ self-interest, goes in the direction of providing innovative responses to alleviate the impact of climate change.
If we do not act quickly, this problem will weigh more heavily on the European economy and, above all, on the lives of European citizens.

Mr President, let us start with a concept: the Mediterranean diet, as numerous studies confirm, helps fight obesity and cardiovascular diseases and is generally very positive for human health in many respects.

The point here is not to try to impose the Mediterranean diet throughout the EU or to try to promote it over other dietary regimes that are better suited to the different climates and regions of the Mediterranean.
We have the task, however, of protecting it, defining its contents and characteristics, defending it from imitation and external contamination that could damage its image and value.
It should therefore be valued like all other European cultural expressions, which deserve to be defended and promoted globally.

It is a model that is being copied around the world, so it is important to define its specific features and safeguard it, so as to prevent a heritage that is completely European from being lost in the sea of globalisation.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the crisis in the car industry has become a crisis for an entire sector, for the supply industry, for other related sectors, for the sales networks and therefore for services, and it has dramatic implications in terms of employment.

I think that the dramatic drop in these latest registrations, which for this month is said to be minus 33% for some Member States, minus 20%, etc., shows that this is not a crisis of a technologically obsolete sector, nor does it originate within the sector, within this or that company, due to managerial errors.
This is a systemic crisis and, as such, must be urgently and decisively addressed by the European institutions.
Some remedies seem to have been identified, but it is important how they are implemented, with what resources and with what prospects for innovation.
We must certainly support demand: consumption is the only lever for recovery.

While we are providing this response, supporting consumption, which is a medium-term response, I think we should say that we need immediate credit support to restart production, pay for materials and support employment, even in the face of a fall in orders and demand.
So, credit is important, but as we said, the way in which it is provided is also important.
I, too, am calling here for Europe to play a stronger role. It is important that the European institutions send a signal.

America is intervening, some European countries are doing so, and I hope that my country will move from generic proposals to concrete initiatives, but I hope that there will be stronger European action in the recovery plan and beyond it.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, reading the text of this document, one gets the impression that the author wants to worsen, not improve, relations between the European Union and Russia.
If this is Europe ’ s objective, then this document is excellent; if not, then it is terrible.
I think it is a terrible document.
How can we think about a future of new tensions with a country whose indispensability we acknowledge in our own interest?
Over the next 40 years, we will have to rely on the traditional energy resources of which Russia is rich. Can we do otherwise?
No.
Secondly, the manner and tone: in these pages, Europe speaks in an imperial language, not the language of someone who respects his interlocutor.
This contradicts our own policy of good neighbourliness and it would not be right to use it even against a small country, still less against a large country that rightly expects to be respected.

First and foremost, it is a question of realism. I fear that the European Parliament is about to adopt a document written in the spirit of the Cold War, which is outdated, useless, harmful and counterproductive, precisely at a time when the new American President is seeking to establish a new dialogue with Moscow.
With this approach, Europe cannot expect any leadership, and I hope that the Commission will not accept these recommendations.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to thank the Czech Presidency, the Commission, the Chair of the Committee on Industry, Research and Energy, Mr Niebler, and my colleagues
rapporteurs of this energy package, the shadow rapporteurs, the Commission secretariat and all its staff for their collaboration and the expertise they have shown in this dossier.
We can and must all be proud of the result achieved. I am, thinking back to the effort made when I inherited the directive on the internal market in gas, with problems that were not easy to solve.

I am pleased that my second experience as a Member of the European Parliament coincides with the final stage of this package, which I consider to be one of the most important files of this parliamentary term in favour of European citizens, our voters.
The Gas Directive, which will come into force from 2011, brings significant innovations to the sector. It is important to stress that the achievement of the option
, which guarantees market opening and real progress towards a system that will truly allow the European Union to speak with one voice on energy matters.
Litho is the real novelty of this package and the one on which it can be said that Parliament has achieved the best result.
The new Gas Directive places great importance on the gas authorities and the Agency.
This directive legitimises the authorities in their role, particularly in those countries where they must start activities from scratch.

It is therefore of fundamental importance to have defined the role and powers of these bodies and to have given them broad powers, since it is the authorities' difficult task to control the common energy market.
Another element inserted during the trialogue is the exemption from the common rules of so-called closed systems, that is airports, hospitals, stations, industrial sites, etc., for
their particular peculiarities. They are subject to a more favourable regime, and this demonstrates the attention that the new directive pays to the needs of the European citizen.

The European citizen is, in my opinion, the one who will really benefit from this directive, since, with the use of smart meters, he will have access to all the information relating to his bill and will be able to assess the best offer on the market and choose his supplier

convenience. While it is true that it will take years to see the effects of this liberalisation, it is undeniable that the entry of new operators into the market will lead to lower prices and more favourable market conditions for EU citizens.
Another important element is the recognition of European transmission networks, which will ensure the security of gas supplies to European citizens.
All of this also requires the reinforcement and creation of new infrastructure, such as regasification terminals and storage sites, which will be the driving force behind the third package.
Accordingly, a competitive market must be opened up, which guarantees investment and long-term contracts for companies in the sector.
particularly in the new Member States, where the creation of new infrastructure would also resolve long-standing dependency problems.
The protection of the weakest consumers has been taken into account, giving national and regional authorities the possibility of guaranteeing them gas supplies at the most critical times.
The successful outcome of the Gas Directive, as part of the entire energy package, once again underlines the role of Europe and its institutions for European citizens.

Once again, we find ourselves in this Chamber defending a food, cultural and rural heritage that comes from a tradition that is centuries old, an invaluable heritage that the rest of the world envies, and that represents a huge asset for our economy and also for the identity of our Union.
a heritage that today risks being seriously undermined by the Commission and the Council ’ s desire to lift the cuvée ban on rosé wines, a desire born under strong

and which will be ratified without the possibility of intervention by Parliament, the body that officially represents the millions of citizens, both producers and consumers, who will be affected by this initiative.

The Commission proposes allowing rosé wines to be made simply by blending red and white wines, as is done in countries that do not have our skills and expertise, under the flimsy pretext of responding to international competition.
As a confirmation of the question, I would like to stress that lowering the quality of our products, which are the result of centuries of tradition and research, of huge investments and passion,
and attention to detail is not the answer we need, particularly at a time of economic crisis such as this, when the average consumer is certainly more concerned about price than about a label.

To counterbalance this decision, it would be enough to find a name for the new product and inform consumers of the existence of two different types of rosé, of the different quality of the products, and of the importance of recognising the work and efforts of the professionals in the sector.
Who will pay for all this?

Does the Commission foresee any support programme in this respect, or will it be the producers who will have to pay the price, after having been asked to fight a losing battle against low-cost competition in already very difficult times?
I hope that the Commission and the Council will make the right choice and, if necessary, completely revise this procedure.
Mr President, let me first of all greet the President of the DTP party, Mr Ahmet Türk, who is here with us in the VIP gallery and who is following the debate.

Dear Ahmet, know that we stand in solidarity with you and that we support the democratic struggle your party is conducting in Turkey for the recognition of the fundamental rights of the Kurdish people.
We recognise the DTP as an indispensable instrument for promoting democracy in Turkey and, for this reason, we firmly condemn the police operations that the
Prime Minister Erdoğan has ordered against you, imprisoning all three Vice-Presidents of the DTP, together with over 300 activists and sympathizers.
The DTP's victory in the last local elections shows that the DTP is a party with strong popular democratic support.

The European Parliament, in its resolutions, has asked Prime Minister Erdogan to open direct discussions with the DTP. Instead, his response has been more repression, more police, more authoritarianism, more prison.

Everyone knows that my parliamentary group and the Left in Europe have supported the accession process of Turkey to the European Union, while at the same time supporting the reform process in that country.

However, I believe that in the last two years, and especially in recent months, Erdogan has shown another face: that of a leader compromised with the worst part of the Turkish army, which simply wants to massacre the Kurds.
The reform process is practically dead, prisons are filling up with Kurds, and it is Erdogan himself who is closing any prospect of Turkey's accession.
It is his responsibility alone for what is happening in EU-Turkey relations and for the growing bewilderment of European public opinion towards Turkey.

I want to send a very strong political message to Mr Erdoğan: either you look for a political, negotiated solution to the Kurdish issue, a priority that must, let us be clear, involve direct negotiations with the DTP, or we will be the ones asking for the suspension of accession negotiations with the European Union.

The road to Turkey joining the Union passes through Diyarbakir and the DTP. Otherwise, it will be we, those who have always supported the accession process, who will have to ask for a pause for reflection that could become definitive.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, it is not my intention, nor that of my group, which supported and wanted this debate, to transfer to the European level the political squabbles of Italian domestic politics.

The issue of freedom of information concerns all of us, it concerns the European Union, which can and must regulate the matter at Community level, in order to counteract political and economic pressures and to guarantee real pluralism of information.

The Italian anomaly, where the Prime Minister is also the owner of some of the most important private networks, consists precisely in the existence of a dangerous entanglement between media, political and economic powers.

That is precisely why we are calling for European regulatory intervention, an intervention that is far from being anti-Italian, but rather serves to protect pluralism in information, avoiding concentrations and conflicts of interest.

For this reason, today we call on the Commission to act as soon as possible, as this Parliament has also requested in the past, to provide the European Union with common rules to protect the freedom of information for all European citizens.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we were in Ramallah when we received the news that Europe had adopted a new decision on the Middle East peace process.
We have seen the hopeful smile on the faces of the Palestinian representatives.

These have been intense days of emotion and suffering, etched on the faces of children clinging to the bars of the windows of their homes, on the road to Hebron, the ghost road that no Palestinian can cross.

The suffering of an 85-year-old woman, driven from her home and forced to sleep in the cold in a tent in the garden of what had been her house; the suffering on the faces of those Palestinians who had built a farm with European funds, only to see it destroyed by Israeli soldiers.
Israel has the right to be an independent state, but the Palestinians also have the right to have a homeland, a state and a passport.
The Council of Europe ’ s decision is a significant step forward, but we must act immediately.
The conflict between Israel and Palestine is the tip of the iceberg of a greater conflict.
On the last day of our visit, the Israeli authorities prevented us, the delegation from the European Parliament, from visiting the Gaza Strip.
I believe that on this issue, the High Representative, Baroness Ashton, should intervene to make the protest of democratic Europe heard.

I would like to thank Mr De Rossa, who led the delegation, and all the other members of the delegation, because during the days we spent in Palestine, the weak of Palestine were able to see and hear the face and words of a Europe that defends rights and wants to do and must do more.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the issue of sound fiscal governance, while always having been of great importance, has become even more topical and significant following the great economic and financial crisis of two years ago.
International summits, the G20 and European summits have discussed it and continue to do so, particularly when dealing with the fight against tax evasion and tax havens.
All of this is important and shows commitment and determination, but we must not delude ourselves into thinking that a mere announcement is enough.
We need a serious and continuous policy.
There are still many problems.
It is still far too easy to buy or set up a shell company to evade tax.
Just have a look on the Internet: there are thousands of sites offering companies for sale, even in EU countries.
Often, all it takes is an e-mail with a scanned copy of the passport attached in order to open a company.
We need to put an end to the practice of creating fictitious legal entities to avoid paying taxes.
This report that I am presenting is based on the European Commission communication on fiscal good governance of 28 April 2009.
The report goes in the direction of putting forward concrete proposals and calls for a strong commitment from the European Commission and the Council to implement these proposals.
The European Union must make the fight against tax havens, tax evasion and illicit capital flight a priority.
This is the basis of the principle of good governance, which is founded on transparency, exchange of information, cross-border cooperation and fair tax competition.
The point is that we need ever greater tax cooperation and collaboration in the European Union.
The overall objective we must set ourselves is to achieve automatic information exchange on a global, multilateral scale, starting, of course, with the European Union.
As Mrs Alvarez has already said, we need to abolish banking secrecy completely in the countries of the European Union and put an end, without further delay, to the derogation regime
temporary measure which authorises the application of a withholding tax at source, often evaded or underestimated, instead of information exchange.

that takes into account the people or companies that have created companies or opened accounts in tax havens; to reconfirm and relaunch projects for tax harmonisation, starting with

the common consolidated corporate tax base. The European Union must also speak with one voice internationally and fight for improvements to OECD rules to move towards automatic exchange of information, rather than exchange on request.

Mr President, I must say that when we talk about fighting terrorism, discussing only intelligence and technology, I always have the impression that we are failing to examine the issue properly, as if we were trying to cure an illness by focusing only on its symptoms and not on its causes.
Is it not time to tackle the problem of terrorism by targeting the heart of the matter rather than limiting ourselves to its external manifestations?
To tackle the heart of the problem means, for example, realising that terrorism is also a direct manifestation of the inability or unwillingness of ethnic groups to integrate into European culture.
I wonder, and I ask this Parliament: do we want to discuss terrorism?
Parliament must therefore have the courage to engage in a political debate on the degree of integration of Muslims in Europe and their willingness to share Western values, rights and freedoms.
In this respect, I have tabled an oral question to the Commission on the whole issue, in order to encourage this House to discuss the matter openly.
It seems that there is no will to tackle the issue.
I wonder, and I ask you, honourable Members of all political groups, if not on these issues, on which others should the political debate of the peoples of Europe be based?
Mr President, the closure of negotiations with Croatia by 2010 is an achievable goal, and the report by Mr Swoboda highlights in a balanced way the progress made and the steps still to be taken.
Justice is now the area where we still need far-reaching reforms.
It is not enough, in fact, to complete the necessary systemic reforms and adopt new laws and cooperate as necessary with the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia.
I stress the need to create a real legal culture and mentality in line with European standards.
The independence of the judiciary is crucial, a central issue, as is the training, recruitment and careers of magistrates, in other words, the absence of any kind of government pressure on judges.
I call on the Commission to consider the need for decisive measures in this respect during the final phase of the negotiations.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, as we know, the European Parliament is being called upon to deliver its final opinion on food information to consumers.
It must be said straight away that this legislative process was objectively arduous, long and controversial, and could not have been shortened.

The aim is to harmonise Community legislation on food through rules designed to make information to consumers transparent, avoiding misleading them into making confused or potentially harmful choices for their health.

The report by Mr Sommer, for which he should be commended for the effort made to synthesise multiple requirements, aims to remedy the controversial and diverse information systems currently in force in the countries of the Union.

It is no coincidence that national legislation still differs in the naming of the nature of foods placed on the market, creating a different system from country to country and also fuelling unfair competition to the detriment of potential consumers.
The proposal from the European Commission has therefore been used as a basis to give new life to existing legislation, involving the food industry on the one hand and consumers on the other.
At the same time, the report calls on the European food industry to clarify the mandatory information and the presentation of nutritional information.

In a globalised market, the European Union could not avoid updating and innovating its legislation on foodstuffs in order to better protect food trade, safeguarding it from increasingly invasive and unscrupulous international competition.

It is nothing new that we hear more and more about the proliferation of food products which, on their packaging, carry labelling and claims that often do not meet food requirements, but which are nevertheless sold as foods beneficial to human health.

using technologies, the network and the various opportunities that can determine these exchanges, starting not so much from a general approach but from individual cases, from the individual problems that magistrates and lawyers face.
that are new and that must stimulate a professional growth of these legal operators within a real incentive to
contribute to the autobiographical reflection on their experience and what they have learned in order to respond to the challenges that Europe is presenting in positive terms.
As you can see, Mr President, Commissioner, we intend to bring something new to the tradition of courses that have been run up to now and which seem to us to be scarcely effective for this purpose.

We need to know how much money is available and what it is for. I have a doubt about the idea, as written in the communication, of a central European institution governing this process, because we are in favour of a bottom-up movement, not centralisation.
This is a new approach.

What we want to say, however, is that unless a European mentality is developed among magistrates and among lawyers, who in this way offer their services to justice and to citizens who request them, we will not make Europe.
So this is not just an important charter for European justice; it is an important charter for the whole development of European unity.
Mr President, the proposal on passengers' rights for those travelling by bus aims to improve and protect passengers, particularly disabled people or those with reduced mobility.
This is the first regulation on bus passengers ’ rights and should guarantee passengers, as happens in the rail and air sectors.
The position in second reading aims to establish a series of rights for passengers who decide to travel by bus.

I agree with Mr Cancian's approach, whom I thank for his excellent work, and I believe that the scope of application of the regulation should be modified by excluding regional services when they are integrated with urban and suburban services.
It would be fair and right to guarantee passengers' rights in the event of accidents, cancellations or departure delays, as Parliament decided at first reading.

The issue of information on passenger rights is equally important. It would be useful, in fact, to provide passengers with information on connections with other modes of transport. This will also ensure dialogue between passenger transport services by bus and those by rail.

As regards disabled people and people with reduced mobility, maximum protection is needed, and I would propose a 24-hour deadline instead of 48 hours for requesting assistance, as well as greater guarantees for compensation and replacement of the equipment that ensures the mobility of people with disabilities in the event of loss or damage.
Furthermore, taking into account the needs of passengers with reduced mobility, any architectural barriers should be removed and existing infrastructure improved to make it accessible.
This regulation aims to improve the competitiveness of the bus sector, promote intermodality, but above all to improve the conditions of travel for passengers.

Mr President, I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr Sterckx, who has done an excellent job on this report, which deals with how to extend free movement in the maritime sector for navigation within the European Union.
I agree with the rapporteur on the purpose of this directive, which is to exempt all maritime transport of goods within the European Union from unnecessary administrative formalities.

Indeed, in order to have a European maritime area without borders, it is not only important that the various competent authorities cooperate satisfactorily, but also that the various simplification systems are applied at the same time.
This is necessary to guarantee the smooth running of the entire European maritime area without borders, and to make maritime transport more attractive and optimally used.

Harmonising administrative procedures between Member States will allow smoother navigation between the various ports of the European Union and will boost maritime freight traffic, which today represents less than 10% of the total.
To date, navigation continues to be hampered by the administrative formalities imposed on it.
Electronic systems should therefore be implemented as soon as possible, because they would allow faster data exchange.

Moreover, interoperability is important for achieving real administrative simplification. Indeed, the sending of information via electronic post is not fully efficient and is useless if the information systems are not technically interoperable.
As for language, I believe that the use of a common language would certainly benefit European maritime navigation.
This would make communication faster, causing less confusion and fewer administrative delays.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to congratulate our rapporteur, Mr Krahmer, because we all know that we have really achieved a result that sometimes seemed impossible.
I would like to point out that this directive, this file, was really considered a file at risk of conciliation procedure.
We have also heard from the Commissioner that it is obvious to everyone that we are faced with a complex job that has taken a long time.
We need only think of a directive from 1996, and this work brings us to the fifth and most important amendment to the Industrial Emissions Directive.
I think that just these few figures tell us all we need to know about the shortcomings and even the flaws in this legislation.

It is a difficult directive to implement – I would like to point out that nine Member States are subject to infringement procedures – and it has such a wide scope of application that it is sometimes even inappropriate.

We must not hide this fact: it has involved great efforts and required sacrifices from all the institutions involved, from all the political groups and from all the national delegations, and has placed us in front of some really difficult choices.
However, I am here tonight to say with conviction that I believe we have achieved the best possible results, safeguarding competition, the environment, and European industrial and employment policy.
On the one hand, we really have a directive that imposes common obligations on all Member States, but on the other hand, we have a directive that also allows for the flexibility that is needed.

I sincerely hope that the results of this revision will be, in the short term, a reduction in administrative burdens and, in the medium term, improved environmental performance and a strengthened European industry.
Mr President, I would like to stress the situation that KISA is facing, a situation that has not been given the importance it deserves for too long.

I am referring to instability in Central Asia, which is unfortunately increasing, while hopes for democracy and economic progress, at least in some of these countries, are proving particularly fragile.

The Kikuyu have suffered in the last month from very severe beatings that will leave, at least in the near future, a legacy of inter-ethnic hatred that will make the stabilisation of the country even more difficult than it was previously.
As has already been said, the international community, and the European Union in particular, must assist the Fijians in three ways as much as possible: through immediate aid for the
emergency supplies and the reconstruction of destroyed homes. Secondly, through economic aid of as long a duration as possible, because as regards the European Union, in the framework of the

strategy for Central Asia and the instruments deployed in that context, through political support to back the process of returning to constitutional legality and the establishment of a genuine rule of law.
The constitutional referendum held in Kiribati on 28 June, in obviously very difficult conditions, can be considered a first step on the road back to constitutional order.
The voting process on 28 June, incidentally, was judged positively by the OSCE observation mission, which took into account, of course, the special conditions under which the referendum was held.
The new constitutional order in Kyrgyzstan will be more oriented towards a parliamentary system than towards a presidential one.

This may perhaps allow better account to be taken of the country ’ s varied ethnic composition and of the need to obtain the agreement of the various components of such a fragmented and composite country.
The European Union and Tunisia are linked by a partnership and cooperation agreement, which provides for regular meetings of a parliamentary cooperation committee.
We hope that our interparliamentary relations can resume as soon as possible with the participation of a democratically elected parliament.
In the short term, as already mentioned, the country needs stability and our political and economic support, so let us not be stingy in either.
Mr President, today we have a Presidency of the Council that nobody voted for, and a government that has lost the confidence of its own citizens.
This alone is enough to understand why Europe is experiencing a profound crisis: a Europe that is now a political paradox, in the hands of a political elite with which citizens no longer identify.
The programme of the Belgian Presidency talks about structural reforms, growth in employment, financial stability and competitiveness, and the chosen instrument is the Europe 2020 strategy.
The current crisis, however, should push Europe towards ingredients very different from those that led to the failure.
A general rethink is needed, because the crisis is not a mirror of decline, but rather the death certificate of the European social model that Euro propaganda would instead administer as a cure.
Europe today is a huge building collapsing in on itself under the weight of democracy, centralism and obstacles to free enterprise.
In order to emerge from the crisis, Europe must change its vocabulary: more decentralisation, less bureaucracy, more concreteness and attention to citizens ’ real problems.
Everything else will only accelerate the inevitable decline.
Mr President, I would like to stress in this short speech that the progressive erosion of European farmers ’ incomes, to the detriment of increasing
profit margins in the agri-industrial sector, is creating a worrying economic marginalisation of those working in the agricultural sector, with consequent closures of the same activities.
A change of course is therefore required, which the European Commission itself recognises and which the report under discussion in this Chamber this morning
the approval of the report this morning, which I wish to support, underlines, with ample motivations and suggestions, also of a legislative and sectoral nature.
We must, in fact, fight global speculation on commodities and guarantee the security of supplies.

Moreover, better price transparency must be guaranteed, which would not only do justice to Italian farmers, but also correct the strong imbalances in bargaining power and abusive debts between the various operators.
Agriculture must re-establish itself as a sector of economic and social stability and regain power in the commercial context of the European Union.
» Apathy and short-sightedness: this is the diagnosis of the malaise that is afflicting Europe 27 today.

We are inactive because one in four young people in Italy is unemployed and there are 16 million unemployed people in the euro area, and also because the euro propaganda of recent months has not been followed by any concretely effective action.

I am disappointed because Europe, in indicating the way out of the crisis, has not only presented the usual recipe, but has completely lost sight of the more authentically territorial dimension of economic and social cohesion policy.

Only by placing the territory at the heart of political action, only by respecting its particular cultural vocation, will European policies on development and employment be able to get going again, drawing on the natural energies expressed by every geographical area: districts, small and medium-sized enterprises, crafts.
In order to solve the employment problem, we must first think small: we must invest in local bodies, promote subsidiarity and safeguard a heritage made up of linguistic and cultural diversity.
If the needle of the European compass does not guide its policy towards the territory, the shipwreck of the euro project is certain.
Mr President, in building a Europe of rights, freedom of information, pluralism of information and independence of information have a central role.
Has the Commissioner noticed that there is a country in the European Union, one of the founding countries of the European Union with the Treaty of Rome, where there is no
the pluralism of information, where a Prime Minister directly or indirectly controls public and private television and the printed word.
What democratic electoral competition can there be in a country where the Prime Minister and a political party control the means of communication?
What kind of democracy can there be in a country where a law is to be passed that does not allow journalists to publish facts relating to criminal proceedings?
other than Watergate, other than a press that is a guardian of the regime, a lapdog? What democratic country is there where the Internet is to be controlled?
What democratic country is there where those who stand in the way of the single prevailing opinion are pushed into a corner and the free expression of thought and dissent is not guaranteed?

We are not describing the Sudan of Bokassa, nor the Romania of Ceausescu, but the Italy of Berlusconi, where unfortunately this is happening, also because the European Commission does not have the courage to put forward an autonomous and free position vis-à-vis the Member States.
Madam President, the completion of the internal market in postal services by the end of this year is an important step, but it would be

It would be appropriate for the Commission to carry out an impact assessment, as some of the Members who have spoken before me have reminded us, of the opening of the postal market, in order to better understand citizens ’ needs.

How does the Commission intend to protect the right of European citizens living in hard-to-reach areas, such as mountainous or rural areas, to receive their post regularly, and what instruments does the Commission intend to adopt to ensure that this happens?
I also believe that this directive, which already exists, should not be exploited to justify the problem of social dumping.
It is the Member States that must apply the directive, and they all have the tools to do so.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, today's debate on readmission agreements cannot avoid the thorny issue of the agreement between the European Union and Libya.
We know that one part of the agreement focuses on combating illegal immigration.
We are concerned because, to date, the Commission has told us little about this, despite the Treaty of Lisbon requiring the Commission itself to inform Parliament in good time.
As is well known, Libya has not signed the Geneva Convention on Human Rights, nor has it so far demonstrated that it is adopting behaviour in line with international law.

I am thinking of the case of the rejected immigrants being held inhumanely in the Libyan centre of Brak, or of the closure of the UNHCR offices, but I am thinking above all of what happened last week, when a motorboat belonging to the Libyan Republic opened fire

against an Italian fishing boat from Mazzara del Vallo, which was in international waters, on the grounds that it had illegal immigrants on board. This is a very serious justification, which makes us think that the agreement signed between Libya and Italy, which

among other things, provides for the return of immigrants to Libya without any provision for the right to apply for asylum or for individual case-by-case assessments of individual situations, and also contains the possibility of using firearms to stop immigrants.

Since in this case too part of the agreement is secret, I would like to know whether the Commission is aware of its content and whether it does not consider it necessary to ask for explanations in this specific case.
the possibility for Libyan authorities to open fire on boats if there is suspicion that they are carrying irregular immigrants, because if that were the case, such provisions would

On the economic level, it is positive for Europe to deepen trade relations with neighbouring countries that are growing strongly. However, the EU has always pursued, at least in words, a trade policy towards third countries that takes into account social and humanitarian aspects.
We must therefore firmly uphold these clauses with regard to Turkey, a country which is becoming more Islamic and where the condition of women is gradually worsening, with more than 1 500
women killed in 2009 in cases of domestic violence; a country in which Christians live in fear of attacks by Islamic extremists, as demonstrated by the brutal murder of Monsignor Padoanese.

In the report, Parliament encourages Turkey to take a leading role in promoting fair and open trade. One thing is certain: we must be concerned about the political leadership role that Turkey is trying to carve out for itself in the Arab world and about the

friendship that Turkey is cultivating with Iran and terrorist groups such as Hamas. In any case, if it is to be trade policy, so be it, but this must never become the Trojan horse to facilitate Turkey's entry into Europe.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, this measure aims to strengthen and rationalise the European Union pharmacovigilance system.
These are the ambitious objectives set out in the Meckeva reports on the amendments to Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 and Directive 2001/83/EC.
Thanks to a long and efficient consultation process between the Council and the European Parliament and the parliamentary committees responsible for the matter, it has been possible
to discuss today a proposal aimed at improving the Community pharmacovigilance system and enhancing the EU ’ s substantial and more than ten-year activity in this area.

Simplification, transparency and rationalisation can be considered the key points of these two reports, which propose measures aimed at guaranteeing the best possible protection of public health on the basis of harmonised standards throughout Europe.

It refers to a wide range of measures, from a better definition of the roles and responsibilities of the main national and supranational stakeholders, to a new and more actively involved patient, who is

recognises its fundamental role in the system, to the strengthening of transparency of information on the safety of medicinal products, and to the simplification of activities relating to the communication, registration and assessment of suspected negative side effects.
The current pharmacovigilance system must be improved, also thanks to closer collaboration at European, national and regional level.
Effective participation at all three levels will ensure a fruitful exchange of information on adverse reactions to medicines.
The new pharmacovigilance system will be able to ensure a high level of shared and secure information in favour of patients, national authorities and healthcare professionals.
Mr President, just in these days here in Brussels the European Youth Event has taken place.

Many representatives of municipalities and regions have discussed how to tackle the serious problems affecting the lives of our cities, on the old and new needs affecting workers, families and small and large productive enterprises.
The economic crisis that has hit our societies has made it harder and more difficult for everyone to provide adequate responses to a new and serious social issue.
In this context, I believe that we all understand that cohesion policy has, on many occasions, been the only tangible instrument available to the regions in their attempts to provide answers.
We can therefore say that cohesion policy has been, in recent years, for all regions, an essential lever for the process of European integration.
We now have to redefine this policy for the period after 2013: we are therefore at a turning point.
For this reason, it is vital to avoid certain risks.
The first, fundamental one is that of its renationalisation: this would be a serious step backwards.

Then, in drawing up the financing budget, we need to ensure that there is an adequate distance, both in relation to the weaker areas and in relation to the ability not to penalise the more competitive and more advanced regions.
Finally, it is essential to simplify procedures for those who have proved efficient and to make the power to impose sanctions more compelling for those who have not.
We expect the Commission to go in this direction.
Honourable colleagues, the road towards a Europe that is fit for women is still long and difficult.
The statistics speak for themselves: the global crisis has hit the labour market, putting female employment to the test, which in 2008 fell by a further seven-tenths of a point.

Despite the provocations that, in the area of equal opportunities and employment, periodically fill an Euro-propaganda that is losing consensus, no concrete measures have yet been adopted to guarantee women true fulfilment as workers and as mothers.
Europe needs to radically rethink its welfare model in the future, and not simply change its label from time to time.

The directive aligning maternity leave throughout Europe is therefore welcome, but the choice to deal with the complementary and equally important issue of paternity leave on the margins of this subject in the same report is inefficient.

Greater integration of women is not only a moral value, but also a strategic objective for the sustainability of the much-publicised European social model, which continues to fail to convince me due to the lack of results.
Mr President, the rapporteur for the Committee on Development, Mr Mitchell, has asked me to replace him, because he cannot be present today.
I thank him for the work done, with his usual punctuality and for the quality of his proposals, which have received the favourable, I would say unanimous, opinion of the Committee on Development.
I am convinced that, on the basis of what I am about to say, but especially on the basis of what Mr Michel has done so far, this report can be adopted by a large majority.
I now come to the report.
In 2009, the European Commission presented a proposal to amend Regulation (EC) No 1905 / 2006 establishing a financing instrument for cooperation with developing countries.
With this proposal, the Commission had asked Parliament to approve an amendment allowing non-governmental organisations to enjoy tax advantages when operating in developing countries.
We accepted this proposal.
However, the regulation on cooperation and development also contains the implementing rules for the European Union ’ s development policy.
These rules provide that when the Commission has to allocate funding, it must follow comitology procedures.

This means that Parliament has the right to examine these funding proposals and, if the Commission oversteps its powers, Parliament can then adopt resolutions asking the Commission to change its decisions.

Since 2006, Parliament has considered that the Commission has overstepped its implementing powers in at least twelve cases, but in only three of these cases has the Commission in fact modified or withdrawn its draft decision.
After the Treaty of Lisbon entered into force, the Committee on Development proposed applying the delegated acts procedure provided for in Article 290 of the Treaty of Lisbon.
Adopting these procedures would mean that, at least in the strategic decisions on funding that the European Commission must adopt, Parliament would have a more important role.
Indeed, according to the Treaty of Lisbon, in certain clearly defined cases, Parliament can delegate to the Commission the power to take strategic decisions. But what could these decisions be?
In our opinion, the choice of countries to which the European Union should provide development aid
It is a choice for the legislator to make: which sectors should be prioritised for funding? Education, health, environmental protection, good governance, the development of small enterprises?
How can we ensure transparency in the management of development aid?
These are the choices and the areas in which the European Parliament should play a more important role than it has had in the past.
It is here that the legislator must give precise instructions to the executive.
This is precisely the direction in which I believe the requests of European citizens should finally go.
I sincerely hope that this report by Mr Michel will receive as much support as possible.
to the rapporteur.

integrated maritime policy, we need to move beyond good intentions and give a new impetus to a strategy that will represent a driving force for growth and development in the European Union, overcoming the huge delays accumulated in the prevention of environmental disasters, in the fight against climate change and against pollution.
This morning, our colleague Pirillo reminded Parliament of yet another catastrophic event that took place in recent days in the city of Paola in Calabria.

The Commission will have to show that it is being realistic, not only by using governance instruments but also by implementing specific legislative measures, learning the right lessons from past mistakes due to an approach that was often too sectoral.

We are fully aware that this process is very complex, but we are in the middle of a difficult and demanding quagmire which cannot tolerate lapses in attention and which requires appropriate investments and
participative governance mechanisms, both at institutional level and in international cooperation for regional basins, of which the Mediterranean stands out due to the complexity of its jurisdiction.
It is therefore essential to view the management of the Mediterranean basin as a common heritage for all the coastal States, based on the principle of

Otherwise, the increasingly widespread practice of unilaterally declaring exclusive economic zones or similar in the Mediterranean, in addition to being contrary to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, would undermine the idea of effective and shared governance.

I would like to recall that only a month ago, this interpretation by Libya led to a real armed attack on a European fishing boat, and it would be wrong to suggest that this is a purely Italo-Libyan affair.

Mr President, I would like to thank Mr Meissner for his report on the integrated maritime policy. I agree with the rapporteur on the need to strengthen maritime transport and shipbuilding and to protect coastal regions, as well as to promote innovation and research activities.
Today, maritime transport plays a fundamental role in the commercial sector.
For this reason, cooperation between small and large ports that face onto the same sea basin should be encouraged, as this can promote a more fluid commercial exchange.
Furthermore, to ensure intermodality, we need to invest in port and hinterland infrastructure, especially in ports that can be linked to European corridors.
Finally, I believe it is important not to overlook another issue, namely port security.

All European ports must have the same safety measures in place in order to better protect maritime transport and to avoid unfair competition to the detriment of those ports that, in order to guarantee greater safety, have to incur higher costs.
Mr President, thirty years ago, Morocco denied for many years that there was a war with the Sahrawi people. Today, it is denying that there is disproportionate repression against the same people.
Yet in Western Sahara there are deaths and injuries among a population that is only claiming the right to live in freedom on its own land.
Morocco has essentially closed its borders to anyone who might be a witness to these events.
No information and no MEPs.
A French Member and our colleague Mr Meyer have been denied access.
Europe is passively watching.
There is no sign of a Commission communication that would make us subservient to the mediation of the United Nations, which has continued to fail as it has done over the last thirty years.
There has been no statement from Mrs Ashton, who is responsible for foreign policy.
Parliament must respond with a statement.
We must react against Morocco by suspending all initiatives on trade agreements, starting with the renewal of the fisheries agreements.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, my speech is not intended to be the usual rhetorical exercise or a polemic, but rather a call for the EU to start paying more attention to the content than to the container, and to abandon propaganda in favour of concreteness.
Having failed in the Lisbon strategy, Europe has come up with a new slogan, Europe 2020, but in practice it has not provided any innovative response to employment problems.

The figures confirm this: 9.6% unemployment in Europe, 20.2% among young people. In Italy, the third largest net contributor to the EU budget, the situation is even more dramatic for young people, with 25.9% of young people unemployed.

In the Europe that I would like to see, there would be no waste of resources in promotional campaigns, but efficient investments so that training prepares professionals for the sectors of the internal market where there are real employment opportunities.
I conclude by reiterating the importance of investing in vocational training, a symbol of excellence in our regions.
The world of craftsmanship offers a potential for employment and a unique heritage of knowledge, which must be entrusted with the task of supporting the competitiveness of the European system.

Mr President, Commissioner, President of the Council, the European desire to set ambitious goals in view of the next climate change conference is certainly a commitment to be pursued with tenacity and with the awareness that the results will shape the future of our planet.
But it is precisely for this reason, ladies and gentlemen, that we must be realistic and more cautious in our expectations.

The international negotiations preparatory to the Cancún conference, as far as we know, have not only failed to make appreciable progress towards a global agreement, but have confirmed mistrust and resistance to the commitments made in Copenhagen.
China has already been mentioned, but I would also like to mention it here.
China, for example, is allowed to increase its emissions by five billion tonnes of CO by 2020, with consequences that could be offset
and perhaps not even that, but a compensation attempt only with a 100% reduction in European industrial emissions by the same year, by 2020.

This figure not only makes the proposal for an unconditional 30% reduction, which we strongly oppose, a mere pipe dream, but also leads many independent observers to hypothesise that global emissions will increase, even significantly, by that date.

In this contradictory situation, it would be at the very least risky and even demagogic to hypothesise figures and sketch out suggestive but unrealistic scenarios, where the unknowns inevitably reflect on the scale of the costs.

We hope that the European Union will bring to Cancún a shared, realistic and sustainable proposal, because an agreement that produces tangible and perceptible results is always and in any case preferable to

expectations that are inevitably doomed to fail and make the efforts of governments on major environmental issues less and less credible in the eyes of international public opinion. This would be a damage that would be difficult to repair.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I have only one regret in this House this evening, and that is that we are discussing the Sahrawis only because the Moroccans have invaded the refugee camps outside Laâyoune. This is a regret for democracy in this Parliament.
Parliament should have developed on its own the will to discuss this issue, which has been festering on international tables for thirty years.
This is the most important political aspect.
We have been forced by acts of violence, not because we have autonomously decided to guarantee human rights.
What is it, if not respect for human rights, that there should be a referendum on self-determination there?
It is precisely because Morocco is our friend, because Morocco is the strong part of the relations, that we must ask forcefully and with all the consequent actions that there be an unpostponable will
We must try to create the conditions so that the MINURSO mission – I am closing, Madam President – is not left alone in creating the conditions for a referendum that is constantly being postponed.
We must try to use all the means at the European Union ’ s disposal to influence this event and to ensure that this referendum does justice to thirty years of suffering by a people.
Mr President, apart from this issue, I would like to raise a problem with the Presidency.
I believe that some people still have not fully understood what the role of the European Parliament is after the Treaty of Lisbon.

I am forced to raise a fundamental issue here: Commissioner Kallas for Transport repeatedly refuses to meet the SkyWay & Space board to discuss the SESAR project, which affects everyone from the point of view of the economy, the environment and safety.

I call on the Presidency to summon Commissioner Kallas to the House and ask him to explain what programme he intends to pursue in this area, which is extremely sensitive and important for the European Parliament.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the report under discussion certainly contains some positive elements. The general approach is to simplify procedures and reduce the bureaucratic hurdles for third-country nationals with a valid residence permit in a Member State.

However, as a point for reflection, I would like to point out that today in Europe, tens of millions of citizens, including many young people, are out of work because of structural shortcomings in the European productive system and as a result of a crisis that does not seem to be ending.

before even helping citizens from third countries residing in our countries, Europe should commit itself to implementing economic, political and social measures that help our citizens above all to find or regain employment.
If Europe can provide its citizens with jobs, it will create solid social foundations to adequately accommodate immigration flows from outside.
The watchword must be, first and foremost, to make Europe grow on its own legs and with its own strength.
Our countries will then have the opportunity to develop their own specific policies.
Mr President, at present, access to employment is regulated only for certain specific categories of citizens from third countries.
Not everyone benefits from the general principle of equal treatment in access to employment.

In this context, the European Union must act to guarantee equal treatment for both third-country nationals legally residing in Member States and beneficiaries of refugee status or of international protection under Directive 2004 / 83 / EC.
We need to avoid a growing demand for seasonal workers, for example, simply because they are paid less or because they have lower costs than a European citizen recruited for the same job.

For example, we must avoid the risk that many multinationals move their headquarters to countries such as Morocco or Turkey and send their staff to work in European subsidiaries because it is cheaper.
For reasons of social justice, guaranteeing equal treatment for European citizens in terms of pay, working conditions and social security is fundamental.
For this reason, I believe it is right that seasonal workers, posted workers, refugees and self-employed workers be included in this directive.
Making differences will become extremely dangerous.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, according to international organisations and police authorities, human trafficking is now the second most profitable activity for organised crime worldwide, after drug trafficking.
It is estimated that around 30 million people are victims of trafficking, prostitution and forced labour.

This means that, first of all, the fight against this criminal phenomenon of very large proportions is a battle for the safety of our citizens, against illegal immigration, against the crime that generates crime.
In the face of a phenomenon of this magnitude, Europe must rightly take drastic measures against those who exploit human beings in various ways.

In the fight against this phenomenon, cooperation between Member States and between police authorities is necessary, but above all, the certainty of penalties for traffickers. Let us remember, however, that many people are

exploited because in their countries of origin they do not find conditions of dignified life, they live in poverty and despair, and are ready to do anything to leave their country in the hope of a better life that will never come true.

Europe must therefore do more and with greater care to ensure that these people can find acceptable living conditions in their own countries, as well as preventing crime and protecting the victims of trafficking.
We must help them in their own countries. This must be the guiding principle of European political action, so that our efforts are directed not only towards repression but also towards prevention.
Okay.

Mr President, Baroness Ashton, ladies and gentlemen, as Vice-Chair of the Committee on Development, I am involved with the problems of many countries, especially in the African region, where human rights are regularly violated, trampled upon and sometimes even forgotten.

I returned only last Sunday from an official mission to the Democratic Republic of Congo, where, in addition to the usual institutional meetings, I wanted to meet the representatives of the NGOs operating in the country and visit some of the centres where they work.
I must say that they also do a very good job. I met the victims in a country where rape is conceived as a weapon of war, where armed groups continue to
repeated mass rapes and other systematic crimes against civilians, especially against women, the elderly and children.
I wanted to mention the example of the Democratic Republic of the Congo because it was a very recent experience of mine.
But the appeal I want to make is generally aimed at the need for a strong and ongoing condemnation of the brutal violations of women ’ s rights.
The international community must significantly increase funding for initiatives to protect women from rape and other forms of
violence, such as female genital mutilation, which is also a serious violation of human rights and of women ’ s physical integrity.
Mr President, the European Heritage Label is of fundamental importance for a continent that hosts most of the UNESCO sites and that
Finally, to highlight those natural, archaeological, urban and cultural sites that have played an important role in the history, culture and integration of the European Union.
The approval of the label is an important step for the process of cohesion of the Union and is also educational and informative for cultural dialogue.
It can also be seen how this choice is a good omen for the new generations, who will increasingly have a shared vision of the European heritage that can unite them historically and culturally.

I would like to thank the rapporteur for her efforts and to offer my warmest congratulations on the negotiations with the Commission and on the success of this initiative, which is so important for the future of the preservation of
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer ’ s, are a health priority for the European Union.
The disease in question has serious implications not only for the health of those affected,
But the costs for direct and informal care are indeed high.
Alzheimer's disease does not only affect those who suffer from it, but also all those who look after the patients.
A European response to the problem is therefore needed: global coordination among Member States, a synergy of concerted actions, especially in the scientific and research fields, and a commitment to

strengthening prevention, diagnosis and improvement of available treatments, including innovative ones, as well as the dissemination of information among Member States on the results achieved within them, in order to increase citizens ’ awareness of the disease.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the situation in Campania, and in particular in the city of Naples, is now unfortunately known throughout the world, and the European Union has had to deal with the issue on several occasions.

In the light of this situation, which is now paradoxical and Third World-like, I therefore ask the Commission what consequences Italy will face from the point of view of Community law and what economic sanctions will be imposed on Italy if the waste crisis in Campania does not find a definitive solution in the near future.

I would also like to know whether the Commission can provide detailed data on the European funds managed so far by the Campania region for the creation of an integrated waste collection and disposal system.
You see, Commissioner, usually when we talk about emergencies, we are talking about dramatic, extreme situations that, however, find a solution in reasonable time.
When a so-called emergency lasts for more than fifteen years, clearly something is not working.

The Italian Government has tried to do its best in recent years to solve the long-standing problem of the waste collection and disposal cycle, but clearly, if today the streets of Naples are overrun with tonnes of waste
tonnes of rubbish, the fault lies mainly with the local Campanian authorities and their total inability or lack of will to find a solution.

It is clear and absolutely beyond doubt that the conditions in which Naples and Campania find themselves are due to the negligence that has characterised municipalities, provinces and regions for so many years in the management of the waste cycle.
If the separate collection, the first essential element of a waste management plan that works, is not carried out properly, it cannot be the central government's fault.

If the Italian Government had to send in the army – it happened a few weeks ago, but also on other occasions – to replace hundreds of nurses who suddenly and simultaneously fell ill, it clearly cannot be the central Government's fault.

If the emergency has hit Naples, while in the rest of Italy things are going quite differently, for example in my region, Veneto, where separate collection has been carried out for years without problems and with a very high recycling rate, it means that the responsibility for the emergency lies

on Campania's policy and on local administrators, and especially on the serious collusion with organised crime, which has always sought and obtained huge profits from the waste business, thanks to the infiltration of the Camorra into politics and local authorities.
After the lies of the Berlusconi government, which in 2008 told the Italian people that the waste problem had been overcome, the European Union is right to...
to block Italy ’ s funding until it presents an environmentally sustainable waste cycle based on waste recycling and composting.

It is certainly not the plan presented by President Caldoro, which is once again based on uncontrolled landfills, such as those at Ciaiano, Taverna del Re and Tersigno, even within the Vesuvius National Park, and on incinerators that produce death and tumours, a real health emergency.
Public funds in Campania, during the environmental emergency of these 13 years, have consolidated the criminal links between politics, both left and right, entrepreneurs and the Camorra.

Environmental issues are resolved by criminalising dissent, by criminalising local communities, families and the land, as the Italian Government has done by using the baton to repress those who defend nature and the land.

According to international law, a political refugee is someone who, fearing persecution on the grounds of race, religion, nationality, social group or political opinion, is outside the country of which he or she is a national.
Cesare Battisti is not a political prisoner.

Despite the protection France has offered him for years, we are talking about a serial killer, the perpetrator and instigator of four murders, who has been hiding behind the pretext of political struggle in order to escape prison.
It is shameful for a large country such as Brazil to deny the extradition of a criminal who has never repented, and to grant him the status of political refugee.
The Italian authorities and, above all, the families of the victims demand that this criminal be brought to justice.
In the face of President Lula ’ s intransigence in denying extradition, the EU cannot leave the Italian Government to its own devices.

Europe has at its disposal and must use all the diplomatic instruments available to ensure that Italy obtains the handover of Battisti, even at the cost of threatening the suspension of the cooperation agreements with Brasilia.
to give tangible support to the Tunisian people and to its transitional government, and to relaunch Euro-Mediterranean dialogue, which has been largely neglected in recent years.
The explosion of anger among the young and the poor in the Maghreb and Egypt stems from the unequal distribution of wealth and the limitations on the exercise of fundamental freedoms.

North Africa is aflame and demanding more democracy, more economy, more participation, more jobs, and is looking with amazement at the immobile Old Continent, unable to grasp the social changes that have taken place in that area and to develop real policies of progress and peace.
Europe should pay more attention to human rights and democracy, promote more dialogue, more collaboration and more real economic interventions.
Europe must open its doors and its heart to North Africa in order to make the Mediterranean a sea of peace.
Enough with the rejection of immigrants and with violent collaboration with countries such as Libya. We need more investment, more reception, more aid and more Euro-Mediterranean policy.
History demands it of us.
The resolution adopted today by a majority on the waste crisis in Campania is a very important signal.
The emergency in Campania has become a European emergency, and this is a decision in favour of the citizens of Campania.
We say no to the allocation of funds that simply go to consolidate corrupt politicians, unscrupulous entrepreneurs and the Camorra, and yes to European money if there is a credible plan, a
a waste plan compatible with the environment, no to uncontrolled landfills in national parks, no to incinerators that produce dioxins, yes instead to recycling and environmentally compatible forms.
and we also say no to the criminalization of dissent and of local communities. So yes to legality.

This is an important turning point, a warning to the Berlusconi Government and the regional Government led by Caldoro, which until now have done nothing but talk and consolidate the cliques and the Mafia bourgeoisie.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the problem of falsified medicines is becoming increasingly widespread at international and Community level, both in terms of the number of cases and the sophistication of the falsified medicines themselves.
It is to these challenges that Mr Matias ’ s report seeks to respond, the result of long and arduous negotiations.
The report, which we supported, has defined a legislative framework which, starting from more precise and detailed definitions, has taken into account
all the stakeholders, the times and the emissions of the pharmaceutical industry, safety, traceability and obligations for the distribution chain, achieving a good compromise.
These are some of the aspects on which we need to work in order to achieve the main objective of establishing more stringent rules to prevent the entry of
of falsified medicines, thus adding to a more positive and complete legislative framework capable of safeguarding citizens ’ health and protecting them from the risk of serious consequences.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, from the virtual square on Facebook to the real square, with millions of people asking for a fairer distribution of wealth, more justice, protesting against corruption.
We must move from the transition of soldiers and armed men to real democracy: a new constitution, new elections, the abolition of the death penalty, more humane prisons, social rights and freedoms.
It will be difficult.
There are those lying in wait who, in the name of an idea, think they can use any means.
We want to believe in the wonderful Egyptian people, who have never experienced democracy.
We want a secular democracy, with a clear separation between religious and political power, a democracy that integrates minorities and guarantees real rights, public health, social security and work.
Europe should support this path, but investing resources that can help Egypt to develop properly: no more dictatorships, no more misery, no more poverty.
Mr President, Commissioner, I think that the Mediterranean issues, with this measure, are entering an even more pressing phase than the previous ones we have taken.
I believe that the act we are adopting today is much more important than the effects of the Community regulation of 2006.

I think this also reflects some of the shortcomings of this regulation, which, being unilateral, has left large sectors of the fishing industry with the impression that, yes, Europe is doing its bit, but the other countries in the Baltic Sea are not.
I think it is strategic for us to address this issue in this way and with these characteristics.
The work carried out by Mr Rivellini, which has been widely supported, deserves a formal expression of thanks in this Chamber.
Nevertheless, I have the distinct feeling that the fact that we are unable to resolve this issue at first reading and that we will reach second reading
and especially on the interpretation of Article 28 of this regulation, raises a question. On this occasion, Parliament sides with the Commission.
Parliament is saying that it is a good thing that these delegated acts should be in the Commission's hands, but there is some resistance from the Council. Today's absence is also a significant one.
However, this act has led to the idea taking root in Parliament that maintaining biodiversity, maintaining the fish stocks of this important sea, is also the future of fishermen.
That said, I think that the phase we are facing must re-propose the idea of co-decision as a central one.
