These delegated acts have their reason for being delegated and therefore conferred today on the Commission, but international agreements must safeguard Parliament ’ s role when they take place.
Mr President, Commissioner, in my opinion, we have two problems here: one is procedural and the other is on the substance of the matter.

The methodological point is that we are using a device, for which we thank Mr Fraga, which is the oral question, to talk about something that should instead be, after the Treaty of Lisbon, a right of this Parliament.
I think that this practice should end: we cannot resort to a strategy to accompany a co-decision process.
Co-decision is such when it is cultivated in the phases in which it ripens.
We are having this debate today, the Treaty will end, it will go ahead and we will say yes or no at the end.
Well, this method needs to be reconverted.

On the substance, however, I would just like to underline, as I agree with many of the things said by my fellow Members, the need to measure the effects of the fisheries agreements, so that they are not just mere trade agreements through which we go and buy cephalopods for one euro a kilo in Mauritania.

They must be able to measure not only sustainability but, above all, the impact on the economy of that country: to understand how many jobs are being created or lost, to understand what level of innovation exists in small-scale fishing, to understand the use of these resources.
It is not just a simple trade agreement, but a fisheries agreement, with all the implications that this entails.
Mr President, the vote in favour of Mr Riva ’ s report is the result of an assessment of the management of the H1N1 influenza pandemic.
in 2009-2010, which aims to establish general standards of conduct valid for any future global health emergency.
The report, following a long and positive phase of re-elaboration, makes cooperation, independence and transparency its main principles.
It calls for greater and better cooperation between the health authorities of the Member States and the European institutions, so as to be able to adopt harmonised and coordinated risk management.
The objectives are to amend the Member States ’ strategy regarding national influenza management plans, including vaccine stocks, the publication of names of
all the experts consulted by the European health authorities, and an efficient relationship of trust with the media, which are responsible for disseminating public health messages.
That is why we voted in favour.

Mr President, until now, the recommendations adopted by the General Fisheries Commission for the Mediterranean have been incorporated into Community legislation provisionally, through annual regulations on fishing opportunities.
These concern conservation, management, exploitation, control and commercialisation measures for fisheries and aquaculture products in the Mediterranean.
However, the permanent nature of these recommendations requires a more stable legal instrument for their incorporation into Community law.
It is therefore appropriate to incorporate the recommendations in question into a single legislative act, to which future recommendations may be added.
Today we have done this, and this will improve legal certainty and at the same time represent an important step towards simplification.
At this point, the contracting parties of the CGMPM have the obligation to ensure full implementation of the agreed measures from the date established.
timely transposition was therefore necessary in order to make these international measures directly applicable to natural and legal persons at Community level and to ensure legal certainty.
Mr President, I believe that the adoption of Ms Podimata ’ s report represents a significant step forward for a more balanced, more advanced and stronger European economic and fiscal policy.

It is now clear to everyone that we urgently need to equip ourselves, as Europe, as a coordination between the 27 countries, with economic and financial instruments capable of governing the economy and not merely of suffering it.
For this reason, the proposal for a financial transaction tax has received a great deal of attention and has been the subject of extensive debate.
The fact that a majority of this Parliament voted for this rule is an important and significant fact, because for the first time we have not let things drift and instead have taken a step forward.
I hope that the Commission and the Council continue along these lines.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I voted in favour of the reports on equality between men and women and on female poverty in Europe, and I am pleased that we are discussing them today, on the occasion of the centenary of International Women ’ s Day.

Despite Directive 2002 / 73 / EC on equal treatment between men and women in relation to access to employment, vocational training and promotion, and working conditions, further efforts are still needed to ensure its proper implementation.

Just think that despite the level of female education having increased greatly in recent years and the number of female graduates being today higher than that of male graduates, women are still concentrated in traditionally female sectors, often less well paid, and occupy fewer positions of responsibility in all areas of society.
The lack of access to care services for dependants such as children, the disabled and the elderly, adequate leave schemes and flexible working arrangements prevent women from working.

I would like to conclude by remembering the women around the world who are fighting for their most basic rights, and I would like our Assembly to express its solidarity with the Iranian feminist associations that are taking to the streets today, calling on women to demonstrate without the veil.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to say to all the colleagues who have spoken that we are finally talking again, and I hope that we will soon start acting again, with specific choices on common economic and industrial policy.
I am grateful to Mr Lange for his excellent report and to Mr Tajani for his communication.

This choice means some important things. First, the recovery and revival of the European economy and the maintenance of employment depend also, and perhaps mainly, on the revival and support of the entire industrial sector.
manufacturing sector, to the more advanced sectors and to the more traditional sectors, because there is a frontier that can still be tackled, and this was not so obvious until recently.

Secondly, the recovery of industrial sectors in Europe also means that our competitiveness is not being played out in the finance and tertiary sectors, but here again, and it is not too late to do so, we can set ourselves the challenge of Europe regaining competitiveness compared to the rest of the world in industrial sectors.

Thirdly, as has been said, the new industrial policy focuses on factors rather than sectors, on the factors and instruments that can really promote new enterprises and new capacity for maintenance and updating.

I think it is important to underline, as has been done, these new factors on which action needs to be taken: credit. We are calling for new initiatives, in addition to those already announced by the Commissioner, on the subject of credit, raw materials and energy costs.

innovation and research, and the need to strengthen Europe ’ s ability to transfer the results of research and innovation into companies, into new products and into new methods and organisation of production.

We have a very special fabric in Europe, in some countries in particular, that of SMEs, that of the quality of enterprises, of the quality of products, in short, of the special nature of European industry.
I think we should rediscover these manufacturing traditions, these manufacturing roots in many countries, in order to innovate and change them.
Finally, there is the very important issue of the territory.
European policies succeed only if they meet a territory that is capable of tackling them.
So, thank you, Mr Lange, and we hope to have excellent cooperation with the Commission to achieve tangible results quickly.
Many goals have been reached, but some battles still have to begin.
Today, the urgency concerns women from other cultures who live in Europe and in the world, in contexts where it is difficult for them to claim minimum standards of dignity and humanity.

We cannot celebrate this festival without focusing on the issue of women in the Arab-Muslim world, otherwise we continue to ignore a problem that today concerns them, but will soon become ours as well.
We cannot ignore the figures on the emergency, which grow year after year, even in Europe, in our cities, in schools where our children grow up.
For some time now, cases of gender-based violence suffered by Muslim women within their own families have been on the rise.

For this reason, I have been asking for some time, and I am asking again today, that Europe implement targeted policies against the spread of Islamic radicalism, against forced marriages, honour crimes, female genital mutilations and domestic violence.
We European men and women must fight for those women who live in Europe, but not only there, who have no voice and are oppressed by the monster of Islamic fundamentalism.

Mr President, I voted in favour of the resolution on Turkey because the constitutional reform that took place on 12 September was unanimously recognised as an important step forward by Turkey towards Europe.
Of course, many steps still need to be taken, and I am concerned about the numerous arrests of journalists.

I would like to stress that Turkey is the seventh largest trading partner of the European Union, that the European Union is Turkey ’ s largest trading partner and that trade, correctly interpreted over the centuries, has promoted the peaceful interpenetration and understanding of peoples.

I therefore welcome the significant progress made in strengthening links between the European Union and Turkey, particularly those via the ' seas highways ' crossing the northern Adriatic and the northern Tyrrhenian.
which now sees a flow of over 250 000 trucks per year in both directions, connected to an important rail link, which carries rail freight wagons from the port to destinations in central Europe.
This has meant that a significant amount of truck traffic has been eliminated from the roads, and I hope that we continue along these lines.
Mr President, today's vote has given a further boost to the definitive solution of this problem, but we have not voted for the best possible solution.

Indeed, I would like to point out that the more than 300 amendments tabled on the report have been turned into 38 compromise amendments, all adopted, which serve to better define both the priority sectors and the objectives of this strategy.

The Commission, with the permanent task force, will have to improve the collection and dissemination of statistical data and best practices, and Member States should appoint a government official – we hope of a high level – to act as a contact point for the implementation of the strategy.
However, we could have been even more, how shall I put it, incisive and less hypocritical if we had focused on the obligations and duties that the Roma community should respect in the host countries.
This is my wish, that is, that solutions be found soon to allow these communities to live together better in all Member States.
Lampedusa has been invaded by tens of thousands of North Africans, whom the island could never accommodate.
Italy has been besieged for weeks by hundreds of boats carrying illegal immigrants.
If we do not all react together, if the EU does not react, the wave of migration will continue.
The clandestine immigrants who have arrived in recent months must be sent home: there is no alternative.
But the Italian Government, beyond a few words of solidarity, has been left alone to manage this biblical exodus of unpredictable proportions. Where is Europe?
Brussels has been largely absent until now; it has had a shameful attitude that my fellow citizens can no longer stand.
Europe has left Italy alone to face an unprecedented emergency.

Europe must not only help Italy to repatriate the illegal immigrants, but must also support the Italian Government in its efforts to prevent thousands and thousands of people from continuing to leave, especially from Tunisia.

The same applies to Libyan refugees, for whom Europe must stand shoulder to shoulder with Italy in identification and logistical management operations, but above all must commit itself to ensuring that refugees are hosted in all Member States.
The reception of those fleeing war cannot be the sole responsibility of Italy.
It is time to stop beating around the bush.
The European Union, if it exists, must act.
We cannot tolerate Italy being flooded with illegal immigrants while France is pushing them back using shameful methods.
The illegal immigrants must be sent home, while all the Member States must commit themselves from now on to welcoming the Libyan refugees.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the issue of providing adequate financial resources to guarantee the decommissioning processes of nuclear plants is a problem already addressed by the

legislation of the European Union on nuclear safety, and it is of great relevance today in the light of recent international events and the consequent decisions taken by the governments of the Union in this regard.

The work of Mr Marinescu, to whom we have expressed our support, deals with the specific cases of certain countries that, at the time of accession, assumed specific obligations regarding the decommissioning of nuclear plants and which received special support from the European Union precisely for this activity.
Beyond the specific cases of this issue, it is necessary to establish an effective system of verification and examination of the nuclear power plants in the Union.
In fact, it seems that Alto is one of the reactors that are expected to be shut down in the near future in the Community.
It will therefore be essential to ensure guarantee mechanisms, particularly in these cases, for the setting aside of adequate financial resources to meet both ordinary and extraordinary needs.
Yes, I would also like to thank the rapporteurs.
The report takes account of the changes in control and communication systems.
This also reveals a picture of great interregional disparities, particularly on the part of the Member States in reporting irregularities and fraud.

The report mentions several times the strangeness of the low levels of fraud and irregularities reported by various countries in relation to agricultural expenditure, Structural Funds and pre-accession funds.
We really need to clarify the statistics that have been provided.
The situation is then made worse by the fact that recovery is ineffective.
However, one fact is clear and confirms a long-term trend.

The fight for legality and transparency in the spending of European resources, as well as the fight against tax evasion and tax fraud, cannot yet count on the full cooperation of the Member States and their governments, together with the European institutions.

In this respect, I think that the calls for a revision of Community legislation on public procurement and the need to have what I call a European public register of beneficiaries of Community funds are appropriate.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I would like to thank the rapporteurs and also Commissioner Barnier for his great willingness towards Parliament.
We support the idea of an internal market that is capable of understanding and meeting social expectations and the implications of the social dimension.
This is not a utopia: it is realism and also modernity.

It is in fact this vision that makes the market itself stronger, because the sole economic factor, economic rationalism in essence, the reduction of the market to economic rationalism, has shown how all this does not solve the problems of growth or those of cohesion.
In short, we need to fully realise the pan-European idea of a social market economy, and we need to do so now.
This is in today ’ s texts.
There is recognition of social enterprises, cooperatives, their role, mutuals and foundations, which represent 10% of European enterprises and a large part of our employment.
These are real companies, a demonstration of economic pluralism and pluralism of companies.

They work with capital, but not for capital, and want to achieve work, welfare, quality and social innovation, and to demonstrate that these values must and can be part of the internal market in Europe.

Mr President, the justification from Beijing that the artist Ai Weiwei has been arrested for economic crimes does not convince us; we believe that this persecution is being exercised against a free and dissident artist.
This arrest is not convincing, as it is part of hundreds of arrests of opponents and the systematic repression of any dissent.

At a time when the Arab uprisings are exploding, our thoughts go out to the image of brutal violence in Tiananmen Square against the young Chinese people who were demanding, as today 's revolutionaries in the Jasmine Revolution are doing, freedom and democracy.
China has opened up to the West economically, but has not combined production with respect for the environment and workplace safety, and has not opened up to democracy and pluralism.

This is not an interference in China ’ s internal affairs, as Beijing claims, but an appeal by men and women, by movements and parties, who intend to build a world free from intolerance on every continent.
The Beijing Government should look at what is happening in the world to understand that, if it does not change, it will be historical events and the Chinese people who change China.
and lost jobs, despite the millions of euros invested in support policies.

This is the difficult balance in terms of impact on SMEs of the Lisbon project, first, and the Europe 2020 strategy, which is now being decided upon, and on which we are even deciding to concentrate the resources of the next multiannual financial framework, 2014-2020.

I can therefore only repeat what thousands of European citizens and taxpayers are asking themselves: what is Europe for if it cannot solve the continent's economic problems, if it cannot speak with one voice on foreign policy and if it squanders money?
I hope that what Commissioner Tajani has said on several occasions will be implemented.
I hope that the revision of the SME Code will really be capable of simplifying bureaucracy, ensuring access to and recovery of credit, and facilitating internationalisation.
The directive on late payment has achieved a tangible and real goal, which is the result of political will, not euro-propaganda.

If the costs of yet another strategy are going to fund nothing more than talk, I call on the European institutions to make amends to the dozens of small entrepreneurs who, in my region of Veneto, have taken their own lives before seeing their small businesses fail.

While Europe is pretending not to see, the 2011 Europol report on organised crime states that with Romania and Bulgaria joining Schengen, the powerful Turkish, Russian and Albanian criminal organisations will be able to expand their activities throughout Europe.
Again, I quote verbatim: 'Illegal immigration from Turkey will spread to the Bulgarian coast, and drug and human trafficking will increase in the hands of the Turkish-Albanian mafia '.

Furthermore, on human trafficking, Romania and Bulgaria ' s accession to Schengen could give the Bulgarian mafia and the new Roma communities new opportunities to increase their already sizeable trafficking towards the rest of Europe.
In short, illegal immigration from Turkey and trafficking in drugs and human beings will increase, and European citizens will be less safe.
Europe wants to do a big favour to organised crime.
The Northern League Party will always be opposed to such outrageous decisions.
Those who vote in favour of this report tomorrow will be responsible for a vote that will open up a motorway for organised crime, leading straight to the heart of Europe.
Mr President, thanks to the Treaty of Amsterdam, environmental policy and the policy of sustainability have become more important and the
attention has focused on the integration between economic and environmental policies and on the inclusion of environmental issues in policies in other sectors.

The key sectors include climate change, sustainable transport, nature and biodiversity, health and the environment, as well as the use of natural resources and waste management, and the international dimension of sustainable development.
In June 2006, the European Council, in its conclusions, invited the Union and its Member States to extend national accounts to include the fundamental aspects of sustainable development.
National accounts must therefore be complemented by an integrated environmental economic accounting system capable of providing fully consistent data.
I voted in favour of this proposal for a regulation because it creates new uses for national accounts data, environmental statistics and statistics in other sectors.
Mr President, my vote in favour of the Garriga report on the Union ’ s next financial perspectives stems from the conviction that, precisely at a time of greatest crisis,
the so-called European spirit, it is crucial to insist and succeed in underlining the indispensability of providing credible instruments, starting with adequate financial resources for the Union.

Therefore, the request for a 5% increase in the next budget is a fair and necessary request. It may not be sufficient, but it is essential in order to respond to the major issues before us. Indeed, these resources are needed to
to tackle real problems, such as greater cohesion between regions, greater research and innovation capacity, and a stronger ability to generate growth and jobs. Indeed, without resources

adequate, none of these problems will find a sufficient response, starting with the introduction of this intermediate category into the cohesion policy, which only makes sense if it does not cause damage to the other existing categories.
That is why I voted in favour, and I hope that the Commission and the Council will follow the recommendations in this report.

Mr President, the new Hungarian Constitution reduces the possibility of intervention by control bodies such as the Constitutional Court and increases the powers of the executive, thus calling into question the normal balance of powers.
Social rights are being eroded: the State only provides benefits to the needy if they agree to carry out public utility work.
The right to self-defence is finally enshrined, which allows people to possess weapons at home even without a licence.
Abortion is banned and strongly discriminatory rules are introduced against homosexuals.
All of this is contrary to Directive 78 / 2000, which establishes a general framework for combating discrimination.

I therefore ask the Commissioner how she intends to intervene, bearing in mind that the fundamental rights of the new Hungarian Constitution are scarcely compatible with those of the European Union and the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we are about to vote on a strategic document of great importance, which provides a solid framework for the negotiations on the financial perspectives after 2013.

starting with the need for consistency between objectives and instruments, between the new role that the European institutions are called upon to play after Lisbon and in the face of recession, and the resources that we decide together to invest.
This consistency also requires a serious response to the demand for innovation emerging from public opinion, from the recipients of EU policies and from European social and productive forces.
We did so for cohesion, a crucial chapter in deciding what we want the European Union to be in the coming years. Experience
tells us that cohesion is today a vital link in the European project, an important asset for everyone, which must be safeguarded, protected and innovated.
I am particularly critical of the effort made on the proposal to introduce an intermediate category of regions, especially because in several cases the recession years behind us have
internal disparities, making it even more important to provide efficient and adequate support for Europe ’ s less developed regions, combat waste and clarify development policy.
And yet, I believe that the challenge of the intermediate categories, with the safeguard clause, is one that we must accept in order to open a new phase in the policy
in Europe, and also to give substance to the more balanced, more sustainable development that we want to ensure for Europe in the coming years.
Mr President, I too would like to join in thanking Mr Langen and the other Members with whom we have worked to draw up this complex report.
I think that what Mr Rübig has said is particularly important.
Mr Langen, when you said that we want to maintain the scope, the field of application of this regulation, keeping faith with what was the mandate of the G20 of Pittsburgh in 2009.
The fundamental objective of bringing the majority of over-the-counter derivatives onto regulated platforms in order to avoid continuing to be exposed to a high degree of
risk and speculation. When, as has happened, we end up trading and insuring USD 400 million without collateral, that is, without adequate reserves, we understand what risks we are running.

That said, I would also like to mention two problems. The first problem is the relationship with the Council. The second problem is the coherence of the proposal for reform of the financial markets that we must carry forward.

I will raise the first issue with regard to the Council, and I will say this very clearly: too often, the feeling is that the Council is an obstacle to doing things quickly and well in terms of reforming financial markets.
I hope that the new Presidency will take up this issue, because even on this occasion we must put into
an extension of the timeframes, which is due in large part to the positions, difficulties and disagreements that are emerging in the Council.
It is a problem that also concerns the procedure that we want to follow.
The second point: the coherence and specificity of the measures.
It is not just the OTC provision; there is also the provision on short selling and others.

I would like to highlight two points: the specific issue of the commodities market, a particular market that requires specific anti-speculation measures, and the problem of local authorities in EU countries being exposed to derivatives.
I believe that these are problems on which Parliament must act.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the Fukushima accident continues to have very serious consequences, with the discharge of radioactive water into the sea at a level 6 500 times higher than the legal limits.
The risk of food contamination is high.
Europe has established very strict controls, but these apply exclusively to fresh products.
No standard has been issued to protect processed products, particularly canned fish.
We import 370 000 tonnes of canned tuna and our main suppliers are the South-East Asian countries, which import fish from Japan.
This means that canned fish imported into Europe could contain Japanese fish.
It is essential and urgent to provide precise information on the place of origin.
Today, consumers know where the can was made, but not where the fish was caught.

The Commission has recently acknowledged this and intends to fill this gap with the reform of the common fisheries policy, which, however, will not come into force for two years, just as the Sommer report, currently under discussion in plenary, will not be effective for three years.
We must not create alarmism, but consumers have the right to urgent and transparent information.
The Commission must act as soon as possible.
Mr Tusk, your speech gives us hope. We hope that the Polish Presidency will contribute to the fight against the Europe of selfishness and
national interests, without a view to the future, but a contribution in favour of an Europe that truly sees itself as an integrated reality with a common destiny.
However, as you rightly said in your speech, the Presidency has real problems to face and solve.
In this short time, I would like to highlight two crucial issues on the economic and financial crisis front.
First issue: reform of the financial markets.
This is not a technical problem; it is a major democratic issue of our time. The timescales are too long. We need to close issues such as that of reforming short selling and OTCs.
Secondly, we are dealing with the issue of financial transaction taxes. We need to get down to business and we expect the Polish Presidency to make a concrete contribution in this respect.
More than twenty years have passed since the Schengen agreements were signed.
No one questions the benefits of free movement, but we must also recognise that the current system is no longer adequate for current and future needs.

For this reason, the Member States and the EU as a whole must commit themselves to revising the Schengen operating mechanisms, so that freedom of movement does not come at the expense of our citizens' legitimate need for security.

As far as we know so far, the Commission's proposals can also be partly acceptable, especially as regards the introduction of a mechanism allowing Member States to temporarily reinstate border controls in the event of an emergency.
We will have to see how this will happen and whether the declarations will be followed by actions and, above all, tangible results.

We will certainly also need to overhaul the European border protection system, especially the external borders, and Frontex must become an agency with real powers to intervene and support national authorities as soon as possible.
We must protect our external borders if we want to continue with this Schengen.
It is time for courageous choices in this Europe, if we do not want to merely survive.

The crisis has deeply affected the real lives of European citizens and businesses, and if we do not act, it risks irreversibly lowering the social and cultural quality, well-being and future of young people and Europeans.

other than cohesion and prosperity values. Getting out of the crisis means finding remedies for the causes identified: remedies against speculation, against which we need to cut the claws by introducing strict financial market rules; remedies

remedies against recession, remedies against an unbalanced model of development that places the individual one step below profit, remedies against the resurgent nationalism that prevents Europe from making the choices that are now its responsibility.

It is time to define instruments and resources, including new ones, to move forward, to get growth going again through research, innovation, training, support for productive investment, infrastructure and jobs, because without jobs people are without dignity and without rights.
This report, thanks to Mr Bresso and colleagues, indicates a possible strategy for a proud Europe that is aware of its role.
European leaders, if there are any, now have the responsibility to implement it.

Mr President, behind the widespread prosperity of our continent lies a hidden Europe, often invisible, which we talk about in terms of statistics and sociology, rather than as flesh-and-blood people, often women, children or the elderly, in other words, the most vulnerable population.
Europe will not be credible to its citizens unless it learns to be consistent, and we want to be consistent here in Parliament at this stage in Europe ’ s life.

If we say that we want to found the Union on values and human rights, I do not think we can so drastically cut or stop a programme for those who do not eat, in other words, for those who do not have the right to food, access to food, and the rights

fundamental points. The legal issue has been resolved: it is an agricultural fund, it is a social fund, and if it is to be social, Commissioner, it must not be to the detriment of other social programmes, but must be a new fund, created ad hoc, with appropriate resources.
We say we want to guarantee food security to Europeans, but the first right to security is the right to have food, which should then be healthy.
This is our consistency and our commitment.

We therefore call for a legal solution that ensures continuity and that, with the help of non-governmental organisations and volunteers, guarantees the first fundamental human right, the right to life.
Mr President, I am supporting this report and would like to underline two aspects in particular: first, because it is part of the current debate on the
review of the common fisheries policy and on the fundamental option of regionalising a policy that has demonstrated all its weaknesses in recent years by being centralised.
This phase of regionalisation finds a particular application in the Black Sea.

I believe that the elements contained in Ms Giannakou ’ s report describe a particular situation, but one that could be repeated elsewhere in Europe. The situation in the Adriatic Sea is no different from that in the Baltic Sea, the North Sea or the Mediterranean.
Mediterranean Sea or the Sea of Marmara, that is, the interactions, above all, between European and extra-European fishing, are often very damaging for European markets and European consumers.
The second point I would like to mention briefly is the call for a common coastguard.
There is no policy if there is no exercise of control.

Today, checks are carried out by disparate forces in Europe. I believe the time has come to understand how one of the first forces, so to speak, of European order, namely the coastguards, can have a common management, greater correlation and, why not, a single European body.
With this in mind, I fully support the contents of Mrs Yotova ’ s report.
Mr President, the disaster that hit the Gulf of Mexico over a year ago prompted the European Commission to reflect on legislation relating to offshore oil platforms.
The European Parliament, too, has wanted to express its position on this matter with this report, approving a text that calls for adequate technical and financial capacity
to the operators in the sector and aims to establish high safety standards, thereby ensuring that the European Union plays a leading role in promoting safety in offshore activities.
The new actions requested of the Commission should focus on international coordination and on a realistic approach combining all the existing excellence
in the sector at the level of procedures and requires the creation of new bodies or additional bureaucracy, which would risk delaying the achievement of the concrete final objectives.

The report, in practice, requires, in addition to greater responsibility on the part of the operating companies, improved communication and information and knowledge sharing mechanisms, and calls for the strengthening and expansion of the role and competences of the European Maritime Safety Agency.
For all these reasons, I consider the result achieved thanks to the negotiations on this text, to which we have expressed our support, to be positive.
I thank the colleagues, Mr Zebre and Mr Skilakakis, and I also thank Commissioner Hedegaard.

I believe that today we are discussing and deliberating in order to really try to achieve a strong European climate policy capable of safeguarding economic development and the well-being of European citizens.
As has been mentioned so far, international climate policies have mainly focused on reducing CO emissions. It is important, however, that this
proposal highlights the need to also tackle the fight against other anthropogenic emissions which cause the formation of tropospheric ozone, which contributes to climate change.

A great deal has already been said, and I think it is right to bear in mind that today we are able to do this at a perfectly reasonable cost. We have the technologies available, and although it is beyond doubt that CO emissions are the main factor

And although it is uncertain that the other greenhouse gases present in the atmosphere are in much more limited quantities, it is uncertain that their potential is very high and that therefore we could really, in a few years, in a few decades, achieve significant results, very
important.
There are signs, as the Commissioner reminded us, in the United States. I have seen a study published in Nature by the scientific agency of the US Department of Commerce, which I find

there are many points in common with the analysis made by the other Members, and so I imagine that these will also be things that will allow Europe to present itself in Durban with an even stronger, more concrete and more important position.

I will just finish by recalling, as has already been recalled, that at this extraordinarily difficult and delicate time that we are living through, we have spoken about the homeless just a few minutes ago, there is a sensitivity among European citizens that we must take into account and therefore, bearing in mind that the great

sensitivity, obviously, to jobs, we should also be able to run a communication campaign to explain to citizens what we want to do concretely in terms of development and their well-being and the protection of jobs.

Mr President, the single market, free movement and the Treaty of Lisbon are all elements that have come into being regardless of any change in our way of legislating, whether at European or national level.

We need to follow up on the commitments arising from these occasions. In particular, we need to really start a phase of simplification of the legislative system, a phase of simplification that must produce a different dialogue between Europe and the individual Member States.

I would start with our documents. I would like to suggest a kind of clarity test for our documents. When we produce a White Paper or a Green Paper, everything must be clearer. I would go so far as to say that the real proposal, perhaps strategic for our Europe, and to really start

this virtuous phase of the start of the legislation, is to assign a specific mandate to a European Commissioner who will specifically deal with clarifying the legislation and the relations between the Member States and Europe itself.

Mr President, Minister, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, first of all, I too would like to welcome positively, as other Members have done, the anti-corruption package that the European Commission adopted on 6 June.
Corruption is a transnational phenomenon and to combat it, European-wide measures are needed.
According to Eurobarometer estimates, the vast majority of our European citizens believe that Europe should do more.
How can we prove them wrong?

In many Member States, there has been a lack of political will to combat corruption: just think that there are still European countries that have not signed the United Nations Convention against Corruption.

If we consider the devastating impact of corruption on democratic processes, on citizens ’ rights and on trust in the rule of law, it is clear that the national anti-corruption measures adopted so far by Member States are insufficient to contain a phenomenon that is on the rise.

It is the European Union that must give a united and strong response, especially now, in the midst of an unprecedented economic crisis that is having an impact on people's lives, on the sustainability of pension and welfare systems and on institutions' ability to guarantee essential services.

Every year, corruption costs EUR 120 billion, equivalent to 1% of the EU ’ s GDP, and daily drains resources from the Union and the Member States that could be used to fight poverty, promote development and protect the environment.
could be used to provide essential services such as education, childcare, employment and transport, in other words, to divert clean resources to support people.
We therefore need more courage and more determination, both in monitoring the implementation of anti-corruption measures and in intensifying and toughening anti-corruption measures at EU level.

In particular, we cannot talk about fighting corruption if we are not able to adopt at least minimum standards for defining the crime of corruption, which is not mentioned in the package just adopted.
The same applies to the definition of uniform parameters for measuring the extent of the phenomenon in the Member States, which is essential for an effective response.
For example, we are in favour of relaunching the network of EU anti-corruption contact points, as proposed in the resolution we will vote on tomorrow.
The crisis is not of yesterday; it has existed for some years.
Perhaps it is time to carry out a critical assessment of the policies followed so far: has everything always been done correctly?
We have always carried out appropriate analyses of the situation.
The right approach has been chosen.
The European Central Bank itself, which, as Mr Rostowski says, has saved Europe, has always done well, even when, fearing inflation, it raised interest rates.
President Barroso has identified a problem: the speed of the markets and the slowness of politics. But is this the only problem?

Certainly, if we consider that we are still implementing the 21 July decisions, this should at least suggest that we should prepare and organise summits and their conclusions better, but politics will always be slower than the markets.
The point is to look further ahead and to shift the emphasis to combating unemployment and development.

This is the ground that the European Commission must choose. Even today, in President Barroso ’ s speech, there was more concreteness on the deficit and less on the indications for growth and development.

The Commission must go beyond its weakness and say that its priorities are fighting unemployment and creating jobs, redistributing wealth and a strategy of public investment, changing the rules of the new Stability and Growth Pact as well.
Madam President, I believe that the challenge for the coming years is to ensure that Europe, as has been said, maintains its leading position as the first,
world-famous tourist destination and at the same time be able to enhance its entire territorial heritage, both in environmental protection terms and in terms of competitiveness.

It is a challenging and far from certain task, especially in view of not only the opportunities but also the aggressiveness of other countries in this sector on other continents, from China to India to Brazil.
Now, in this context, as Commissioner Tajani pointed out, it becomes essential, as also highlighted in the opinion we adopted in the Committee on Regional Development,
to combine a strong overall strategy by the whole of the European Union on tourism with an appreciation of the role of regions and territories.
In this respect, it is essential that regions and local authorities are fully involved from the outset, in line with the principle of subsidiarity.
Furthermore, I believe that we should encourage the development of integrated tourist networks and projects, starting with the creation of partnerships for the exchange of best practices.
I also think it is right to propose the creation of a Europe label that can define a high quality tourist product.
It will obviously be up to the Commission to better define the priorities, especially from a financial point of view, in order to ensure that Europe maintains its leading position in the future.

Mr President, the preparation of the joint strategic framework for research and innovation funding represents one of the greatest opportunities that European development and the European economy have before them.

We are in fact talking about acting on the lever that can really trigger a period of growth and also of new qualified employment, such as that of researchers and young people who are devoting themselves to the new opportunities in technology and related professions.
One observation that emerges from this work in particular is that we must increasingly promote research and innovation in Europe that lead to practical applications, that lead to
to give rise to new companies, to new patents, and not to remain mere basic research, mere knowledge, which is important, yes, but not to the extent of really triggering the desire for development.

The second point is the relationship with local authorities, the regions and the territories, which are also an important tool for programming at local level and for bringing together research, universities, centres of excellence and businesses.

Mr President, European schools were created with the aim of guaranteeing access to education in the mother tongue for the children of European institution staff who are required to work and live abroad, in a cultural context different from that of their country of origin.
The first European school was created in Luxembourg back in 1953, at the initiative of a group of officials.
Over the years, given the positive results of this form of teaching, 14 European schools have been created in the various Member States.

While recognising that the system of European schools is a necessity rather than a luxury, it is clear that, more than 50 years after the creation of the first European school, this system may have some outdated aspects and should be adapted and updated in some way to meet new economic and social needs.

I therefore voted in favour of Mr Cavada's text, which calls for greater involvement by the European Union in supporting and coordinating the actions of Member States, which are called upon to act in order to develop and improve the system of European schools.
And investing, we know that investing in education means investing in the future of our young Europeans. Thank you, Mr President.
Mr President, over the past few decades, the number of natural disasters that have killed tens of thousands of people in various parts of the world has increased exponentially.
Last year, within the Committee on Development, I followed the work on the report on the European Union ’ s disaster response capacity, in which
emerged, how much still remains to be done to ensure a coordinated response from the European Union to the increasing number of earthquakes, floods and tsunamis.
The European Union ’ s response to the Haiti earthquake, for example, clearly showed the complexity of the existing instruments and procedures.

Even though the Member States and the Community institutions acted promptly in sending humanitarian aid, the crisis management has raised a number of problems in terms of efficiency, coordination and transparency.
That is why I voted in favour of Mrs Gardini's text, which calls for greater efficiency in all phases of disaster management, greater coordination
between the parties involved and simplification aimed at avoiding unnecessary overlaps and time-consuming procedures that hinder the provision of rapid and effective aid.
This is just a good initiative. I think we will then have to build a European policy on tourism.
The European label is a good idea, but I would like to dwell on two issues: there is no such thing as sea or coastal tourism without a policy for the planning of coasts.

I believe that the time has come for Europe, precisely in order to protect this important sector, to launch an initiative in which the concept of the sea and the concept of the coast are not limited to a kind of urban planning that ends at the high-water mark and does not involve the sea as a whole.
I believe that an integrated policy in this sense would be useful, as would building up everything that lies behind the claims made for rural tourism. Rural tourism can be
an excellent source of income for disadvantaged areas, for areas where there is no longer any work, where it is important that depopulation does not continue and where human settlement is maintained.

Well, there it is not enough just to make statements, but we should suggest that Europe and the individual states intervene on the fiscal lever: doing tourism in rural areas means perhaps paying fewer taxes. Perhaps this is the way to really create a real opportunity for tourism and for those who live in the countryside.
Mr President, I would like to congratulate Mr Fidanza on his work, which is logically placed within the framework of the actions
taken and promoted at Community level in the field of tourism, following the assignment of new powers to the EU in this area by the Treaty of Lisbon.
I agree with and wish to support the statement on the importance of Europe as a unified destination for certified and quality tourism, and the desire to promote this
same idea, translating it into actions at the level of structures, resource allocations, funding, and coordination of actions between the various levels of government of the countries of the Union.

This is an approach that should be encouraged and rewarded, given the importance of this sector in terms of its contribution to GDP, employment and new opportunities for small and medium-sized enterprises.
and the valorisation of the environment, is able to develop and thus respond to the main challenges posed by current times.
For all these reasons, I consider this report to be positive, an important signal in the direction of promoting a European policy for tourism.
Mr President, I have three brief points to make. We need more Europe here too.
I believe that on certain issues it is inevitable that we adopt an approach in which we do not just hope for more Europe, but actually build more Europe.
More Europe means, for example, really starting to think about a common road police and identical rules in terms of safety protection.
More Europe means having a European training policy, not simply delegating this to individual states, but building, for example, a real training policy
towards schools, towards young people, and towards certain categories that are more at risk. We mentioned motorcyclists a moment ago, but we could also talk about truck drivers.
More Europe means having common instruments in vehicles that improve safety conditions, such as alcohol and drug detectors,
sleepiness. I believe that a specific strategy should be developed with the car manufacturers and with European vehicle production.
I believe that if all this is incorporated into the conviction of a common policy, then perhaps conditions will improve.

Mr President, this report deals with the issue of global food security. Global means that this issue is not just about world hunger in poorer countries, but that the issue of food supply affects all communities, including those in the most developed countries.

It outlines some guidelines, such as the essential one of consistency between European policies and this objective of global food security, because we are an institution that preaches very well but is often inconsistent in its choices.
I would just like to highlight three other innovative lines that I believe we should develop further.
The first is that food supply is today threatened by price volatility due to speculation on foodstuffs, which must be contained, if not prevented, derivatives, etc.
Secondly, the issue of land grabbing, which I think should be the subject of some kind of treaty or negotiation, because it puts democracy and the survival of many peoples at risk.
Thirdly, consistency between supply and energy policies.
We cannot, in producing new energy, prevent food from being produced for the peoples of the world.
Mr President, I voted in favour of this report by Mr Zimmer, first of all for a reason of principle.
purely political nature, because I think it is necessary to bring the great issue of food security back to the centre of the European Union ’ s initiatives.

It is not just a matter of helping developing countries to deal with this issue more effectively, but the food crisis, given that it is expected that by 2050 demand for food will increase by more than 70%,

agricultural products. This is a major issue that, if not addressed in time and decisively, will become one of the main threats, if not the most important threat, to stability, peace and also democracy, as we have seen.

For this reason, I believe that the entire European Union, together with the Member States, must make every effort, starting with adequate financial investment, as indicated in the report, in order to seriously tackle this major issue.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I really must thank all the people who have contributed over the past few weeks and months to the production of this document that we are voting on today in plenary.
We have worked very well and a truly constructive debate has developed.
The motion for a resolution we are voting on today is therefore the result of truly joint work and the contribution of all the political forces and of the whole Parliament.
I believe that a very strong common awareness has developed on this issue, also because we must remember that we live in an age that is truly characterised by an exponential increase in disasters.
The figures, if we look at them in their stark reality, are very telling: the number of disasters in the world has quintupled, and if we consider that in 1975
There were 78 catastrophic events recorded, whereas today we have almost 400 disasters a year.
Over the past 20 years, unfortunately, 90 000 people have died and more than 29 million people have been affected by disasters, while the economic losses have amounted to EUR 211 billion.
It is clear that we are facing phenomena of such dimensions that require a stronger response, a more effective response and a really strong commitment on our part.
We all have in mind the images of the earthquake in Haiti, the floods in Pakistan, what happened in Japan.
Europe itself experiences floods and fires on its own territory every year, and we remember the ash cloud following the eruption of the Icelandic volcano.
There is no need to go any further to affirm how important the work being done here in Parliament is, following two principles:
that are our guiding stars: the principle of solidarity, reinforced by the solidarity clause in the Treaty of Lisbon, and the principle of subsidiarity.
We are moving along these lines.
The European Union has always acted generously and effectively in the past, as I believe the indefatigable work of our Commissioner Georgieva demonstrates, but today we need to make a quantum leap.
a quantum leap that we are ready to make, to move from an ad hoc response to a response where the reaction
be strengthened through the preventive identification of the means already available in the Member States and made available to the European Civil Protection Mechanism.
It is also important to stress that, in order to make this leap forward in quality and to move from sharing information and responding to emergencies to a role of planning and monitoring,

coordination, and we welcome the fundamental role of services such as the Joint Research Centre. I would like to take this opportunity to express our concern about what is happening with the budget for these new technologies, which are fundamental.
We have included the importance of communicating with citizens, because the Japanese experience has taught us a lot about this too: it is never sufficient, it is never enough.
We await the Commission, to which we ask to present the proposal on civil protection in legislative form as soon as possible.

Ethnic minorities oppressed, Christians forced to keep their faith secret, priests barbarically killed without the authorities lifting a finger, an anti-Western foreign policy: this is today's Turkey.
My party, the Northern League, has always been opposed to Turkey joining Europe because it represents a potential Trojan horse for radical Islam in our continent.
Today more than ever, we are convinced that the EU must bring an end to accession negotiations.
The intolerable threats that Turkey is making against Europe must be met with an immediate response.
We cannot accept that a third country tells Europe what to do.
Cyprus is a full-fledged EU Member State and has an indisputable right to assume the rotating Presidency.
Europe must send a clear signal and show itself ready to defend Cyprus by all means against the arrogance and political and military bullying of Turkey.
We should immediately suspend the accession negotiations and be ready to suspend trade relations as well.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, we are going through a decisive stage for the future of the euro and, by extension, of the whole of European integration.
Having this awareness would mean moving away from a myopic logic that subordinates everything to an ideological desire for budgetary austerity.
We all want the various states ’ accounts to be in order and we all want to reduce indebtedness.
However, it is clear that without an intelligent investment policy capable of breathing new life into the economy, there will be no possibility of growth.
The only clear result of this approach is that, as always, the heaviest prices will be paid by the most vulnerable groups.
For this reason, together with the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament, we voted against this report.
Indeed, we are continuing in one direction, that of austerity without growth, a direction that has so far proved to be wrong.
I very much appreciate the work done by Ms Ferreira in this report.
Indeed, on the package of six legislative proposals dedicated to economic governance, only on this one did my group and I express a positive vote.

We did so because, among other things, a clause on the protection of social rights, starting with collective bargaining, has been included, and because the role of Parliament in adopting delegated acts has been strengthened.

Therefore, although we cannot change the overall balance of the package of six proposals, this report certainly takes us a step forward towards, among other things, a surveillance system that takes fundamental rights into account.
Therefore, in this context, while maintaining that a vision based solely on austerity does not produce positive results, in our opinion, this report is certainly a positive note against the trend.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, Finland and the Netherlands opposed accession in the Council for a very simple reason: Bulgaria and Romania are not currently able to guarantee effective border control and are the scene of rampant corruption involving the political class and organised crime.
It is not populist parties and movements that think this way, but Europol in its 2010 report on organised crime.
There is no populist propaganda here, but a serious security issue.

If Bulgaria were to enter Schengen at this time, the police and security bodies predict that the Bulgarian borders, particularly the maritime ones, would become an easy target for Turkish and Eastern European crime, and Bulgaria and Romania would become crossroads for human and drug trafficking.

It is natural that some Member States have many reservations about the real ability of Bulgaria and Romania to guarantee effective border controls, and to combat organised crime and corruption, and it is therefore absolutely natural that they have opposed their accession to Schengen.
Allowing this now would pave the way for transnational crime and open a gap in Europe ’ s borders, precisely where there is very strong migratory pressure from Turkey.
There are many reasons for this, and Europe cannot ignore them.
I would therefore like to know what the Commission and the Council intend to do about this delicate issue, which in my opinion has so far been dealt with in a very superficial manner.
sufficiency and without taking into account the dangers that the accession of Bulgaria and Romania would entail, according to the competent bodies in the field of security and international crime.

In my opinion, the Commission and the Council should postpone the entry of these two countries into Schengen and only give the green light when there is reasonable certainty that the entry of Bulgaria and Romania will not have repercussions on the security of European citizens.
Mr President, the crisis in Europe is entwining the problems of public budgets and private capital with those of the productivity of our enterprises, and the spectre of recession is there.
If SMEs are therefore a priority, I ask the Commission to ensure, together with Parliament, that our good principles are implemented
and that the legislative initiatives of the Small Business Act become a concrete reality in the Member States, with a high level of efficiency and quality.

Think, Commissioner, of what would happen if all the Member States were to apply the Late Payments Directive ahead of schedule: what a great advantage it would be for SMEs. SME tests are important, but we are still at the stage of methodology.

Of course, it is right to verify whether, when we legislate, we are really doing so in the interests of and to suit small and medium-sized enterprises, but we are asking that you proceed with great consistency and tenacity, in depth, in creating this framework of opportunities that you have drawn up.
We appreciate what is being done, including by the Commission, and we are ready to support it with the budget and with the other possibilities we have.
I will just say a word on credit.
Let us be clear with the banks, I say to you, Commissioner, because we need to help small and medium-sized enterprises.
Mr President, I voted in favour of Ms Wallis' text.
without prejudice to the usual ways of accessing the courts, I am convinced that an alternative dispute resolution (ADR) mechanism represents a quick and inexpensive alternative to legal proceedings.
In addition to being an effective tool for small and medium-sized enterprises, ADR can also develop significantly online for the resolution of minor disputes.

Through joint action with the Member States, we must therefore promote the creation of a harmonised legal framework which, by strengthening citizens ’ confidence in the internal market, can help to stimulate the European economy.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the report on the provisions for actors placed on the market under the flexibility scheme, to which we have given our support, is based on a measured and realistic approach which is

realized on the one hand by avoiding the setting of implausible and impossible-to-satisfy requirements, and on the other hand by establishing a so-called flexibility regime, which, by providing for time phases and corresponding quantitative limits,

emissions allowed, provides for the annual release onto the market of a pre-established quota of tractors meeting certain characteristics and thus allows the phased implementation of the pre-established standards without causing any trauma to the productive and industrial sectors concerned.

Mrs Tarabella, the work you have done is important and you have managed to strike a careful balance between the requirements dictated by the major challenges that the European Union is called upon to face at this time and those of environmental protection and the current economic crisis.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the current global economic crisis, which is even more serious than that of 1929, has caused unsustainable imbalances in the international economies in recent years.
Trade imbalances between countries, financial markets lacking common rules and the instability of banking systems are all potential threats to financial and economic stability.
This resolution, which I support, stresses the urgent need for a new set of rules and a new global economic framework.

The economy needs a global governance system, open markets, high economic and social standards, a more responsible monetary policy and stricter supervisory policies, and the decision-making bodies and international economic organisations must be the main players at the heart of this new approach.

Europe in particular needs to be more transparent in order to reduce the instability exacerbated by globalisation and to guarantee far-reaching reforms aimed at promoting growth and reducing the debt of individual states.
This is the commitment that this resolution underlines to all countries.

Mr President, I voted in favour of the report by Ms Matera, which analyses the situation of single mothers, who today represent, as has already been said, more than 5% of the population of the European Union.
The European Union therefore has a moral duty to protect them, and the text approved today supports policies that can provide tangible help for these women, such as, for example, tax breaks for
healthcare, the possibility of flexible working arrangements to reconcile private and professional life, priority access to affordable housing and special unemployment benefits.
Only by adopting these measures can we respond to the needs of a category that is becoming increasingly significant in all developed and industrialised countries.
Mr President, the budget sets out a few clear and important objectives within the framework of the EU 2020 strategy, so I would like to thank the rapporteurs.
It is essential to focus on investment and not just on prudent budget maintenance.
The alternative would be to passively accept the speculative philosophy that characterises our time.
We can no longer afford to follow our individual interests, but must finally represent a single, compact entity ready for confrontation.

I welcome the commitments made on pilot projects and preparatory actions under heading 2, as well as the funding for the integrated maritime policy, provided that this does not come at the expense of other initiatives and programmes in the fisheries sector.

This will allow us to implement new policies aimed at better management of marine resources through research and widespread exchange of data on fish stocks, introducing new approaches that can be used in the Adriatic area as best practices in other marine areas.

I will conclude with a small postscript. In the context of future debates on the CFP, and in particular on the fisheries fund, I hope that more effort will be made on fisheries control, which is at the heart of the fight against illegal fishing, but also the basis for rebuilding stocks.
that fish be considered a common good.
Mr President, the economic, financial and social crisis we are going through has clearly highlighted various weak points in the European economic and social model.

I believe that one of the most alarming and representative figures of the current situation is the almost six million jobs lost in the last three years, figures which are bound to increase if we do not react promptly and effectively.

Ms Bastos's text underlines that in order to make tangible progress, we need better synergy between education, training and the labour market, together with policies aimed at ensuring greater correspondence between demand and supply in that market.
For these reasons, Mr President, I voted in favour.
I voted in favour of the report by Mr Angelilli because it tackles an issue that I am deeply concerned about as a mother and a grandmother.
Protecting children from sexual exploitation, abuse and the disgusting world of paedophilia means defending our children and grandchildren and, consequently, defending the most vulnerable.
Greater prevention, tougher criminal action against those who commit such crimes and more careful protection of children are the key points of the document voted on today.
The road ahead is undoubtedly a difficult one.
I am thinking in particular of the spread of child pornography on the Internet, which can only be combated through close cooperation between Member States, police authorities and providers.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the challenges posed by modernity and the report by Ms Zanotelli, to which we have expressed our support, require us to place the citizen, in his role as consumer, at the heart of the matter.

at the heart of the Community debate, and they remind us that safeguarding its interests cannot remain merely a principle enshrined in the Treaty, but must be a concrete guiding principle of the European Union ’ s policies.

A protected and informed consumer, made more aware and therefore able to fully exploit the tools and resources at his disposal, able to fully enjoy his rights, must be the ultimate goal of the European Union ’ s new strategy for consumer policy.

This strategy should therefore be an all-encompassing, far-reaching approach, covering everything from the financial services sector to the food sector, from the protection of minors to advertising, and thus capable of making an important contribution to Europe's social and environmental objectives.
Mr President, the online gambling sector is expanding continuously, with a turnover of several billion euros and many issues to resolve.
I therefore fully agree with the adoption of all measures aimed at safeguarding the integrity of sport, including greater coordination between Member States and the various sports federations.
Last year, together with Mr Iacolino, we tabled an interpellation calling for a firm intervention by the Community institutions to prevent fraud in rigged sporting events and to

to combat the possible interference of organised crime in European sport, requesting, as in the text voted on today, a common definition of sports fraud and harmonisation of penalties at European level.
For this reason, I voted in favour today.

We hope that this is the role of the Commission. I say this as a representative of a country that is changing its government at the moment, and it is not a technocratic government. A technical government shows that the political forces have not given up.

The policies to combat the new and old poverty, which unfortunately are increasing in today's Europe, in the Europe of the crisis of the last few years and also of austerity policies, one-way, especially on the part of the States,

financial commitments, new resources, both on the European budget, within the framework of the financial perspectives, on European policies, as was just mentioned, from the European Social Fund to the FEADER, and on the budgets of the individual Member States.

Above all, on the individual Member States, we should use the European Semester to monitor, year after year, concretely the policies that the individual Member States are devoting to this fundamental challenge.

In order to make credible the objective of reducing poverty and social exclusion for around 20 million European citizens, it is therefore necessary to have consistency between the huge resources that Europe must make available and the concrete policies that we can put into practice.
It is estimated that around EUR 2 billion will be needed by 2020 to achieve the objectives we have set ourselves.
This is the challenge that we, as the Committee on Budgets, highlight and place at the heart of our attention: consistency between the financial resources we make available and the policies we adopt.

In the light of this situation, we are here to discuss plans to support the Roma communities and to provide accommodation for immigrants.
We must think about helping those categories that are really in difficulty, and also give financial and political support to the family, which is the institution on which our society is based.
We must help small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) that are being strangled by the crisis, because these SMEs are the real antidote to poverty and the real source of wealth in our countries.
We must give priority to our best people, not tax them to guarantee migrants with a minimum income that Europe wants to impose from above.
