Mr President, I voted in favour of the text on the work of the ACP Joint Parliamentary Assembly, which, thanks to the quality of the work carried out, has established itself as a key player in cooperation between the North and the South of the world.
As a member of this Assembly, I myself attended the meetings held in Brussels and the work carried out in Tenerife in April 2010 and in Kinshasa, in Congo, in December of the same year.

It is thanks to these visits that I have been able to see first-hand, as I did in Angola in 2009, the sad conditions that certain peoples live in on a daily basis and, at the same time, to assess the efforts made by the ACP to improve things.

In conclusion, I would like to underline one particular point, at a particular historical and political juncture, especially for the countries of North Africa and the Middle East, namely the role played by the ACP has been one of

of fundamental importance, particularly in the supervision of negotiations for economic partnership agreements, promoting transparency in these processes and the development of disadvantaged local communities.
Madam President, I personally find the creation of a European Heritage Label interesting.

At a time when, because of the persistent economic crisis, there is a certain degree of Euroscepticism in many Member States, I believe that an initiative of this kind can strengthen citizens ’ sense of belonging to the Union.

The hallmark of the European Heritage Label is not the beauty of a site, but the symbolic value that it has represented or represents for the integration of European citizens on the path towards better intercultural dialogue.
Mr President, in December 2009 I attended the Copenhagen COP 15 as an official member of the European Parliament delegation.

It has been about two years and, with regret, I can say that the work done and the commitments made in Copenhagen and then formalised in Cancún will not be sufficient to achieve the objective of limiting the increase in the average annual temperature of our planet to two degrees Celsius.

Some major polluting countries, such as Japan or Russia, seem to want to distance themselves from the commitments they have made, while others seem not to give climate change the priority it deserves.

Today, even though I am expressing my vote in favour, I am aware that only with the great and firm will of the Heads of State and Government in the world can we lay the foundations for a comprehensive international agreement.
Services linked to the Internet still have a lot of potential, both for the economy and for a more participative and inclusive society.
Today, in the phase of transposing the telecoms package into national legislation, there is a risk of fragmentation of the European framework.
The Commission's monitoring role will therefore be important, and I would also say the BEREC role, which some of us would have liked to see strengthened in the definition of the telecoms package.

The priorities we want to reiterate in this debate are: guaranteeing network neutrality, developing broadband infrastructure and developing more open software, security of access and interoperability of systems, promoting the use of the

digital technologies in the various industrial sectors and in services, production of digital content and new services, inclusion of those currently excluded – I am thinking of the disabled, but not only – and the defence of pluralism in information and culture.
It will also be provided and guaranteed that operators cannot block, limit or discriminate against users, and that their transparency in commercial offers and in all their activities is maximum.
Mr President, we all know how important the role of the International Criminal Court is, which deals with prosecuting perpetrators of war crimes, crimes against humanity and genocide.
Unfortunately, in recent times the Court has increasingly been the target of unjustified political attacks.
Its arrest warrants have often been ignored, and as a result, the current budget is not proportionate to the efforts and commitment made.

I therefore voted in favour of the text adopted today because it is the European Union's duty to take all political, diplomatic and logistical action to support the Court, guarantee its independence and ensure that it functions correctly and effectively.
Mr President, phosphates in detergents for domestic washing machines end up in water systems, in European rivers, streams and lakes.

Phosphorus accelerates the reproduction of various forms of life. As a result, algae and other fast-growing plant species proliferate excessively when there is an increase in phosphorus in an aquatic environment.
This excessive growth of algae deprives other living organisms of oxygen and can also produce toxins, destroying fish and plant species and thus reducing the quality of the water itself.

I therefore voted in favour of the text adopted today because, in order to clean up Europe ’ s water resources, it is desirable to reduce the amount of phosphorus that is discharged into the waters of our continent.
Mr President, the report presented by Mr Lisicki analyses the possible measures to be taken to reduce defence costs, which weigh heavily on the Community budget in a time of financial crisis.
The main proposals concern better coordination of defence system planning, pooling and sharing defence capabilities, and support for research and technological development.
I therefore agree with the rapporteur's position that the economic and financial crisis that has hit us could represent an opportunity for integrating the
policies of the European Union, setting in motion the process of developing and finally implementing those ambitious reforms that have been at a standstill for years.
For these reasons, I voted in favour.
Mr President, following the Arab Spring and the events that followed, it has become necessary to revise the European Neighbourhood Policy.

It is clear that, given the current state of affairs, relations with the countries concerned should be more focused on cooperation in order to guarantee that democracy and security are given priority on both sides of the Mediterranean.

On this point, I believe that intercultural and interreligious dialogue is fundamental to strengthening mutual respect and tolerance with our neighbouring partner countries, while at the same time promoting the fight against terrorism and extremism.
For these reasons, Mr President, I voted in favour.
Honorable Domenici, thank you, Mr. President. Yesterday in Florence, a matter of extreme gravity occurred, which causes horror and dismay. A man
A 50-year-old man, armed, opened fire in two markets in the city of Florence on five citizens of Senegalese origin. Two of them died and three are now hospitalised in critical condition.
The murderer then committed suicide.
This is the extreme racist and xenophobic right wing, which wanted to kill people who were guilty only of having black skin.
Florence is my city and, before being elected to the European Parliament, I was mayor of Florence for ten years.
Florence is a civil and democratic city, awarded the Gold Medal for resistance to Nazifascism.

Yet it was in Florence that this racist abominable crime took place, demonstrating that Nazi-fascist madness can manifest itself anywhere and we must never think that it has been definitively eradicated.
Mr President, I ask you to express the condolences of the European Parliament and to reiterate its commitment to fighting all forms of racism and to respecting the rights of every human being.
Mr President, the management of projects in war zones such as Afghanistan cannot be directly compared to the management of projects in
developed countries or in countries not affected by conflicts, with aid that is clearly hampered by the unstable security situation.
We must, however, try to understand why the shortcomings highlighted in the management of the projects and their related risk factors exist, in order to improve the situation as much as possible.
I agree with the rapporteur's position in calling on the Commission to carry out a more careful assessment of fraud, corruption and lack of effectiveness in specific projects.
We therefore need to increase transparency and accountability in the use of Union funds and help both Member States and other donors avoid common errors.
Mr President, we do not have time to go into the details or to discuss the matter in depth, but I believe that we must acknowledge that there are some points that remain very critical.
I share the concern about this Green Fund and the question of where we will find the resources remains a big question mark and a big concern.
However, I believe that, to be realistic, we must recognise that this time things have changed in Durban.
The old approach has been overcome, as it no longer reflected the real geopolitical situation.
We continue to work on a picture of the world of the 1990s, an old picture, which, if we look at it in perspective, projected towards 2050, is truly unsustainable.
We see that by 2050, 19 of the world ’ s 30 largest economies should be countries that are still on the list of developing countries today.
I think that this change of pace is perhaps the most interesting thing from a political point of view, and our colleague, Mr Florenz, has indeed underlined this very clearly.
I also think that we should no longer proceed with compartmentalised approaches.

Then, remembering what Mario Draghi, the President of the European Central Bank, said the other day in this very institution, if we want to move forward in this sector towards our priorities, that is,
We must be realistic and focus primarily on the technologies of the green economy in order to achieve real sustainable development and to respond to our citizens ’ most important needs.

Madam President, Europe is shrinking, stock markets are crashing, the centralist Europe of the financial lobbies is falling apart, but the Danish Presidency is opening its term promising total loyalty to the Commission's plans for the creation of a super economic government for the continent.
Instead of focusing on the measures to be taken to save the livelihoods of 500 million citizens, the Danish Government promises to act as a loyal watchdog of Euro technocracy in Council.
Other passages, however, I do not agree with at all.
First of all, I do not see how we can today propose as a priority the acceleration of the EU enlargement process towards the Balkan countries.
There are other problems to be addressed immediately, before worrying about how to integrate countries in Europe affected by corruption and crime.
Then there is Turkey: the Danish Government is strongly pushing for Turkey ’ s accession.
Turkey is, fortunately, a long way from meeting the criteria set by the EU for accession.

But the real paradox is that, at a time when the euro is at risk of failure, it should be the Danish Presidency that saves it, Denmark being outside the euro area, because it has evidently never trusted the euro.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the increase in violations of intellectual property rights recorded in recent years
years, risk undermining the strategy on intellectual property rights in the single market launched by the Commission in May 2011.
To counteract this phenomenon and safeguard the success of the policy for the protection of intellectual property, it has been proposed to entrust this unique
tasks relating to the management of the European Union Observatory on Counterfeiting and Piracy, established in 2009, to the Internal Market Harmonisation Office.

This is to provide the Observatory itself with human and financial resources to undertake actions to combat violations of intellectual property and awareness campaigns aimed at increasing consumer awareness of the risks associated with purchasing counterfeit products.

The vote in favour of the report is therefore a very important moment in the fight against the trade in counterfeit goods and in the defence of innovation, growth and competitiveness of small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as of employment and of the security of European citizens.
Mr President, the property in question may seem contradictory in its definition, but the reverse is true: it is very coherent.

I voted in favour of this proposal because its purpose, which is precisely to entrust the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) with certain tasks relating to the management of the European Observatory on Counterfeiting and Piracy, is necessary and indispensable in order to combat infringements of intellectual property rights.

The main objectives are to increase awareness among public and private sectors regarding the impact of counterfeiting and piracy, and to inform consumers about the dangers associated with purchasing counterfeit products.
On the basis of the 33 compromise amendments, an agreement was reached at first reading on 19 December.
The amendments provide that the title of the regulation and the name of the Office should not be changed in the sense of the European Observatory.
Furthermore, the important and fundamental thing is the participation of civil society in the meetings.
All of this will make an event such as the one we are celebrating with today ’ s vote even more democratic and participatory.
Mr President, Mrs Lechner ’ s report is part of a series of actions aimed at improving services for citizens and businesses in the European Union.
The interconnection of central and commercial registers is indeed an excellent tool for making communications and information retrieval easier and, above all, faster for citizens and businesses.
Therefore, Mr President, I voted in favour.
Mr President, every time the situation seems to improve, new downgrades from the rating agencies come to shake up the international markets.
Many European companies are in crisis and on the employment front the number of people, especially the younger ones, chronically looking for a job is growing.
If austerity plans and the defence of the euro are necessary measures, even more so are the profound revisions of the labour market at national level, aimed at relaunching the European economy.
Only by boosting competitiveness will the European Union restore investors' confidence and promote the creation of new jobs.
I do not deny that we need to protect the rights of homosexuals. What I deny is that in Italy, as it is, there are no same-sex families.

homosexuals will never exist simply because the family to protect, the one that even the European Parliament should protect, is the traditional family, the one made up of a woman, a man and their own children.
That is all. I think it is a fairly basic thing.
This is the reality in Italy and in the other 22 countries.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to join in the warm greetings to Mr Lechner and to the assessment of his work, which he has carried out with competence, tenacity and determination.

The European Union should be proud to have such an approach at its core.
The solutions that have been proposed are intelligent and new solutions: for example, the person making a will has been given the choice between his habitual residence or his country of origin.

This makes a decision more flexible, given that there are such profound differences, really very profound differences, between one state and another, which could constantly cause problems with the issue of succession.
However, legal certainty has also been provided, because once the legal solution has been chosen, either habitual residence or country of origin, it cannot be changed.
This limits the discretion of judges and also limits the conflicts that may arise, so that things can proceed more peacefully in this area, and so there is more freedom and more certainty.

Then there is the certificate of succession. We believe that the certificate is of little importance, but it creates the conditions whereby the heir has greater flexibility and certainty in the face of all the possible difficulties that may arise.
This is a fact that positively characterises this law.

It is true that this law is not perfect: there are problems relating to the executor of the will, less practicability of direct inheritance and so on, but I just want to say one more thing of a political nature.

the United Kingdom in a different position, because Europe cannot do without the United Kingdom. We will therefore have to proceed to overcome these difficulties.

Mr President, the European Union has produced an enormous amount of legislation and standards to build European law and has created the European area, known as the Stockholm Programme.
There is a mismatch between this corpus, this legislative monument, and the use that citizens make of it, which is numerically and proportionally very low.
On the other hand, national judges and national lawyers in the various Member States have grown up within the law of their own country and have a predominantly national training.
whereas we believe that the real European judge is the national judge who changes his mindset and becomes imbued with knowledge of European law.

Therefore, before talking about training, we need to talk about a major communication campaign aimed at citizens and legal operators, so that they become the promoters of this major innovation and increase their ability to want to take advantage of this right.

But above all, it is a bottom-up movement that must be created in in-service training. We cannot lecture in judicial training as if we were teaching schoolchildren, but rather adults who are highly professional.

We need to encourage curiosity in dealing with our own cases, to network with other colleagues and to find solutions, given that today common law and civil law are converging, they are no longer as far apart as they once were within the legal culture.

That is the real point. In our proposals – and I am pleased that the European Commission has become convinced of these aspects – in our Convention, we are calling for a reversal of the trend: bottom-up, more legal studies than judicial training.

There is an example in the Netherlands called Eurinfra, which is very interesting: it is a network of people responsible in each court who spread this knowledge and encourage the magistrates to do likewise.
This is the substance of our proposal and we hope that it will have the full support of the Commission.

Mr President, I would like to begin by expressing my sincere appreciation for the work done by Mr Giegold in this own-initiative report, and I would also like to recall that, together with him and others, I am an active member of Parliament's Intergroup on the Social Economy.

As you know, my political group believes that the social model is one of the founding features of the European integration project, so not just one of many policies, but a policy that characterises our project.

In these times, with attacks also linked to the need for cuts in public spending, and so on, we still defend this priority with conviction. We know that the model can be updated and modified, but we also believe that it must be maintained, defended and reiterated as a priority.

Therefore, I believe that in this same vision, we must also value economic operators who run businesses and produce goods and services, such as cooperatives, which play a very special role in the so-called European social economy. These are businesses that

show that it is possible to produce, to do good things, to be efficient, even if profit is not the objective, but for example, employment, welfare, the enhancement of work and contribution to the life of the local community.

That is why it is important – I am concluding, Mr President – that Europe provide the tools needed for an active policy and that we have a good statute for cooperatives, not the one that is currently in place, which is unworkable, but a good statute, which could be a way of helping this very important sector.
Mr President, the role of the cooperative movement has always been important in building a more balanced and fair economic dimension.
At a time when we are going through one of the worst economic and social crises since the Second World War, the role of cooperatives is crucial. It is clear that where there is

a robust development of the cooperative dimension in the economy, there has consequently been an increase in employment, more highly qualified work and a more balanced redistribution of the wealth produced.
It is therefore positive that the European Union has adopted a statute for cooperatives, but now more than ever it is necessary that there be a real simplification of the rules.
We need to give a new impetus to the creation of a common European framework in the field of cooperation, and this must be done by helping, not by creating bureaucratic obstacles.
For this reason, I voted in favour of this resolution and I hope that the Commission and the Council will act actively to resolve the problems mentioned.

The survival of millions of unemployed Europeans does not depend on the meagre assistance offered by the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund. The data collected show that the majority of the beneficiaries of the Fund are unable to find new employment, while the more fortunate are reintegrated into the labour market with fixed-term contracts, sometimes lasting only six months.
In many cases, unemployed people give up on this fund because they doubt its usefulness.
The FAE is not a solution, it is a band-aid for the disastrous consequences of a European trade policy managed dangerously and with too much lightness by the Commission.

In order to combat the scourge of unemployment, which is now structural, Europe must put a brake on its excessive openness towards the markets of developing countries, whose unfair competition is the primary cause of the failure of our companies.
Until Europe stops promoting economic self-harm by defending a neo-protectionist stance towards global trade flows, our companies will continue to fail.
The fund can only provide temporary support, but it is thanks to training courses that our unemployed people will find peace of mind and security.

Mr President, I believe that today is a great result for the European Parliament, because on 15 July, the Council wrote to Parliament saying that it was ready to accept the Parliament's position on the regulation on the European Globalisation Adjustment Fund.
Mr Buzek, when you said that we should set up an anti-mafia committee, I remember that many of my fellow Members from different countries said: 'But the mafia is a problem for Sicily and Italy'.

Today, the fact that our colleagues from the north of Europe have acknowledged that there is a problem of organised crime in Europe and that they have the will to fight it together, I believe, is a very important fact, and I would like to say thank you, Mr President, and thank you to the whole Parliament.
Yes, thank you, Mr. President. Thank you, Mr. Sondar, for such a comprehensive presentation.

First of all, I would like to reiterate our solidarity with the families of the victims of the serious clashes that took place on 16 December in Zanuzan, as well as our concern about the subsequent developments.

What began essentially as a trade union protest degenerated into very serious incidents, in which 17 people lost their lives, and into the subsequent arrests of journalists and opposition representatives.

It is in everyone ’ s interest, and as Kazakhstan itself has demonstrated, to shed full light on the events, and I hope that today ’ s debate will be a positive opportunity for our Parliament to look into what has happened so far.

and to reiterate our appeal for an investigation that will reassure the international community that the responsibilities for the events in San Jose will be clarified and that those responsible will be prosecuted, while fully respecting the principles of legality and the protection of human rights.

The delegation I have the honour to chair had the opportunity, during some of its meetings, to listen to explanations provided by the Kazakh authorities and also by some very active NGOs that monitor events in this country.
The willingness of the Kazakh authorities to provide explanations, as well as their promises of further clarifications, are undoubtedly positive elements that we must all appreciate.

For this reason, I would like to stress the need to maintain a certain balance, a balance that I would have liked to see highlighted in the text of the resolution that we will have to vote on tomorrow, and which, in my opinion,
in my opinion, it might have been more appropriate to wait until the official investigations were concluded, to which it would have been useful to add an official mission by international bodies.

The undeniable need to clarify what has happened and the need to appeal for ever greater efforts to be made by Kazakhstan to improve progress on the road to democracy must always be accompanied by a renewed commitment to a constructive dialogue with this country.

Mr President, I voted in favour of the present proposal because it aims to update the 2004 and 2009 regulations in order to adapt them to changes in national legislation in the Member States in the field of social security, and to keep pace with changes in social reality that affect the coordination of the social security systems themselves.

The present proposal also includes proposals from the Administrative Commission for the Coordination of Security Systems, whose purpose is to improve, modernise and acquire in full compliance with all that established in Article 72, letter f, of the previous regulation.

The changes contained in this proposal are intended to guarantee legal certainty for the parties concerned and also to ensure appropriate coordination at European Union level of national social security systems.

It also reflects the legislative changes that have taken place at national level and is necessary to approve it because it takes account of recent developments in cross-border mobility that affect the coordination of social security systems.
Mr President, the credibility of the European Union on the international stage and the effectiveness of its human rights policies certainly depend on condemning violations and abuses

committed in the context of, or outside, armed conflicts, of the lack of respect for women, children and, in general, the weakest, and of persecutions based on religion or belief. It is also of vital importance to avoid disparities

of criteria between the European Union ’ s human rights policy and other external policies, thus promoting democratisation and supporting the fight against social inequalities, particularly in times of economic crisis such as the current ones.
Mr President, I would like to thank my colleagues, Mr Zannis, Mr Patrão Neves and Mrs Romeva i Rueda, and their assistants, for their substantial contribution to the success of this initiative.
Fish is a common good, as it provides 15% of the proteins for over three billion people and the livelihood of over 500 million people depends on fishing and aquaculture.
This makes it a genuine global public good.
Adoption of this declaration sets out a clear path for the Commission on the need to promote responsible conduct, both among fishing communities and in consumer behaviour.

The debate on the reform of the common fisheries policy has, from today, one more reason to support actions that take European policy out of its narrow focus on the methods of harvesting resources and on a set of rules that are all inward-looking.

On the contrary, a strategy is suggested for the sea as a preferred location for increasing its reproductive capacity, both in internal waters and through innovative methods of fisheries agreements with third countries.

Moreover, all this is not only about safeguarding the environment and maintaining biodiversity, but also about responsible fishing, economic and social sustainability, and the specific interests of fishermen and our economies.
Mr President, the third amendment to Regulation (EC) No 1049/2001
1083 / 2006, tabled by the European Commission a short time ago, contains provisions aimed at creating a European instrument for sharing risks.

The objective is, in fact, to address the difficulties encountered by some Member States in raising the private funding needed to implement investment projects that can be financed only partly by public funds.

Although the road to overcoming the economic crisis is still long, I believe that the work done to reach a compromise text, which has set a ceiling for the funds to be allocated to the financial instrument and better defined the risk-sharing instruments, has been fruitful.
Mr President, the economic crisis, technological development and demographic changes have transformed society and the labour market in Europe in recent years.

In order to meet future skill requirements, many people will have to complete higher education, and many workers will have to continuously update their skills and knowledge, even when they are already employed.

For this reason, I voted in favour of Mr Takkula's report, which aims to modernise higher education systems in order to achieve the objectives set out in the Europe 2020 strategy and to promote social, cultural and economic development within the Union.
Mr President, I will not go into great detail, because in the short time available I would just like to mention a few things.

I believe that this seventh environmental action programme can certainly prove to be a useful tool, but only if it is based on a vision of the future that takes into account the need to maintain a solid European industrial base, which is essential for maintaining a decent and acceptable level of employment.
There are some things that worry me.
I believe that an absolute priority is that this framework, this programme, ensures the more effective implementation of existing legislation, even before setting

further binding targets. I am deeply convinced that, in order to meet today ’ s environmental challenges, we must base future environmental policies on solid evidence and the most up-to-date scientific knowledge.

I am concerned about paragraph 24, which talks about the ecological footprint as an absolute indicator. It is an indicator, an empirical model of reality, not reality itself. We must bear that in mind.

This has many limitations: it tells us how much we touch the environment, but it does not estimate, for example, the quality of the environment at all. Therefore, my concerns remain, and I call on everyone to take them into account.
When we talk about good examples for citizens, when we talk about the internal market and the possibility of creating products,
real and tangible opportunities for change in everyday life through the internal market, we can really use this example. We will obviously need many more examples as a result of our work.

reduced, but because they can increase the supply of services and, in effect, also help the economy as a whole. So, I think this is certainly a positive example, a good example.

However, Commissioner, you will have understood that there is a widespread desire in Parliament – I refer in particular to the speeches by two of my colleagues, Mr Martini and Mr Goebbels, from my group, but also many others –
others who would like this good example to become excellent and for us to move towards, and this measure partly provides for it, a zero roaming result, because it is possible.

They belong to an old left-wing conception," etc., etc. Others said, "It doesn't work, and so on, it hinders the market." But in the end, we did it.

Perhaps among the many benefits, this extra-wide expansion shows us that the possibility of using it without real fear of costs also helps democracy.
In Europe, there are more than 120 million broadband users, at least 70 million citizens who use their mobile device to connect to the Internet, to work, study or simply to inform themselves.
Thanks to this regulation, the European Union has finally decided to intervene in favour of all these consumers by reducing the cost of international phone traffic, voice, data and SMS.
A positive decision because the price reduction programme will lead to an average tariff reduction of 35% over the next three years.

This, however, is not enough. In a difficult time such as the present, both economically and in terms of employment, communication, new technologies and innovation are primary goods that Europe must defend with greater courage.
Every European citizen must be able to move freely between Member States, enjoying the telephone tariff set by their national operator.

Enough with fraud and exorbitant bills for those who travel in the EU. If we really want to give a concrete response to citizens, we therefore need some kind of Schengen for telecommunications, which no longer imposes different tariffs on citizens depending on the countries in which they operate.
Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I was part of the European Parliament delegation that went to Sicily in November last year.
Our head of delegation Cecilia Wikström wrote a letter to the Italian Minister, the Minister of the Interior, Mr Cancellieri, and we received a reply which gave us some fairly clear answers.

I would like to stress the fact that, after that visit to Italy, the government changed and the approach to the situation changed too, but the situation itself also changed in the countries from which the migratory flow mainly originated.
This should not, however, prevent us from carrying out a careful analysis of what has happened and of the current situation.
The most relevant fact that is still current today is the status of Lampedusa as a non-safe port, the closure of the first-aid and reception centre in Contrada Imbriacola following the fire at the
September 2011, which damaged it, but only partially: one dormitory destroyed, one damaged out of seven buildings in the structure, and then the de facto cancellation of the project

Presidium. Leaving aside the fact that in the Mediterranean the only unsafe port was Tripoli under Gaddafi, and I do not think this comparison is appropriate, seeing that migratory flows, although considerably reduced in number, also due to seasonal reasons,

First aid and reception activities must continue to function in Lampedusa, which is the first port of call for those arriving from the other side after journeys that are far from easy and not without risks, as the Somali shipwreck victims who arrived

in April of this year, after seeing ten people die on the boat they were travelling on, or the 20 who were transferred from Lampedusa because there was no room in other reception centres, or the 18 who are still living in the changing rooms of the stadium in Lampedusa.

The tourist residence in Cala Crasta where they are currently housed is certainly not a CIE or a CARA, and meanwhile, in the meantime, both the health system and the possibility of applying for asylum have been dismantled in Lampedusa.
Everything is left to the goodwill and kindness of the Carabinieri on duty or of the citizens and volunteers of Lampedusa.
Another point to consider is the role that the centre has played in the recent past.
Allowing thousands and thousands of people to amass in a place that, due to its size and intended function, could only accommodate a few hundred, in defiance of every legal and ethical rule, and

passage. Allowing a territory of just a few square kilometres, already difficult because of its position and logistical problems, to be forced to host a number of people greater than its own inhabitants must have caused a great deal of concern.
reflect on the responsibility for a situation of extreme tension that then led to
I believe that the fire itself
I believe that Mr Rinaldi's excellent report was successful in the Chamber.
I absolutely agree with Mr Lulling's assessment of the report.

Nevertheless, I would like to raise a small point: our Europe, in relation to this new world that is trying to find democracy and different economic conditions, is a Europe divided by the Mediterranean.

It is precisely the Mediterranean that I would like the Commission to pay particular attention to, both in terms of environmental aspects and in terms of the economic transactions that take place in the Mediterranean.
The Mediterranean is a fishing ground.
We are building a new fisheries policy for Europe, which will set very strict rules on the part of Europe.
On the other hand, however, there are no rules.
So why not use fisheries agreements to support these countries so that they apply the same rules as those applied in Europe?

This would be beneficial for European fishing, beneficial for the sea, for the conservation of the environment, and for making sure that this split is less and less of a split, but is more and more of a homogeneous place on which to build the future of Europe and of this new world.

With the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the European Parliament has become a full-fledged institutional player in the field of security policies and has therefore the right to actively and decisively participate in the priorities of the internal security strategy and the Union's security model.

Borsellino's report identifies five key sectors for which concrete actions have been proposed and calls for greater judicial and police cooperation between Member States in order to combat organised crime and terrorism.

I believe that it is particularly important to give high priority to the development of prevention policies, which are fundamental instruments for guaranteeing a space of freedom, security and justice in Europe.
Mr President, with the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the foundations have been laid for the development of criminal law integrated into Community law.

While recognising the importance of mutual recognition, harmonisation of protection standards and the principle of the presumption of innocence, it is important to stress that the law must be extremely clear and easily understandable, so that everyone knows what actions constitute a criminal offence.
This is particularly true of the directives that must be reciprocally transposed into national law.
Any lack of clarity could lead to divergent interpretations of the law.
I therefore believe that only close cooperation between the European institutions on the principles and methods to be followed can guarantee a consistent European strategy of quality in criminal law.

Mr President, the European Union protects the general interests of consumers against unfair business practices, misleading advertising and unfair contract terms, but there is no such protection for the interests of consumers in the area of energy supply.
There is no doubt that those consumers who are more vulnerable due to age, health problems, physical or mental, or simply due to a lack of adequate information, should be protected more carefully.
I therefore voted in favour of Mrs Pérez's text because I agree that the creation of a strategy for consumer rights
so-called most vulnerable people, contributes not only to their social inclusion, but also to guaranteeing a safer, fairer and more competitive single market.
Mr President, the report by Mr Pérez Álvarez, which we supported, deals with the need to consider the development of a
strategy aimed at protecting the rights of the most vulnerable consumers, in view of the strategy that the European Commission and the Member States will be called upon to draw up in this regard.
Consumer vulnerability stems from causes of different kinds and potentially affects everyone.

For this reason, it is necessary to provide protection for this category in addition to the general protection of consumers, and to draw up a targeted strategy that, taking into account the complex nature of the problem, avoids

On the one hand, the formulation of static and rigid definitions that are unable to consider all the facets of the issue and the various possible forms of vulnerability, and on the other hand, the prevention of the creation of new forms of discrimination.
It is therefore necessary to add to the definition of specific and targeted protection measures.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, the management of bluefin tuna fishing has a long and rather complex history since the 1990s, and is probably today the most strictly regulated by the European Union.
Despite the huge investments in controls having led to greater compliance with the rules, there are still concerns about overfishing.
I therefore supported the Romeva i Rueda report, which proposes a reduction in the allowable catch rate, greater monitoring and stronger measures to reduce capacity.
Mr President, the relations between the European Union and China clearly show imbalances in trade.
It is therefore incumbent upon the European institutions to make every effort to help European businesses emerge from the crisis and to achieve fairer access to international markets.
I therefore expressed my support for this report, particularly agreeing with the rapporteur on the application of the principle of reciprocity to
relations between the European Union and China, in order to ensure fair competition on international markets, and the call for greater protection of intellectual property rights.
Mr President, the road is still long, but today could be an important step in the direction of finally introducing and applying a tax on financial transactions.
I understand the caution, the prudence and the need for gradualism expressed by Commissioner Šemeta, and the technical problems that Minister Vestager rightly mentioned.

But precisely for this reason, I hope that Parliament will express a clear political will, with a large majority, to give impetus and momentum to the necessary initiatives to tackle the problems that remain open, particularly at Council level.
We all want to assess the impact of this tax carefully, but it is not right to stir up unwarranted fears, which could lead to contradictory and counterproductive decisions.
I am referring, for example, to the logic behind the proposal for exemptions.
If we are concerned about reducing the impact of the tax, then it is important to have the broadest possible tax base with a low rate, rather than extending exemptions without limit.
For those concerned about the impact on small investors, I would point out that the tax is progressive, so that those who carry out more transactions and more
transactions, and it is clear that the competent authorities will have to monitor the situation in any case to ensure that any costs are not passed on to customers and end users.
I therefore hope that Parliament will vote by a large majority in favour of this tax.

Mr President, since 1990, through the macro-financial assistance instrument, the European Union has been providing economic aid, in the form of grants, loans or a combination of both, to countries experiencing difficulties with their balance of payments in the short term.
The rather long timeframes, added to the difficulties highlighted by the decision-making criteria used so far, have made it necessary to draft some amendments to the regulation.

I therefore welcome the proposal for a new regulation, which aims to make the evaluation criteria faster and more effective and to agree clearer rules and conditions for the granting of macro-financial assistance.
Mr President, there are around 100 million European citizens involved in various forms of volunteering.

From major sporting events to cultural events, volunteers play a fundamental role, and we must not overlook the work of those people who rush to provide aid and solidarity in the event of serious natural or environmental disasters.
Volunteering is one of the broadest expressions of active citizenship and a crucial activity for the development of democratic values and equality within the European Union.
For this reason, I hope that we will develop high-quality volunteering, both nationally and cross-border, and that we will remove the obstacles that currently exist.
Mr President, from the communications sector to finance, transport and energy, and from culture to health, technology is playing an ever more important role.
The ever more frequent use of the Internet and digital media makes the issue of cybersecurity one of the priorities on the European agenda.

Information and communication technologies can, in fact, promote economic and social development only if the current rules guarantee users' security in terms of data privacy and intellectual property rights.

Only full international cooperation involving Member States, institutions and the industry sector can bring to life a solid system capable of introducing effective measures to guarantee information security.
Madam President, Commissioner, thank you. You said some very important things, which I very much agree with.
I find it truly, as he said, shocking that we have all experienced, each in our own country, the figures and statistics that we heard earlier from Ms O'Kane.
However, it is equally clear that this scandal has really highlighted the need for a careful assessment of the current European legislation on medical devices.

Unfortunately, this fraud has highlighted the loopholes that we have discussed here in the Chamber and that the Commissioner has so ably described, so I would not like to dwell on this aspect in the short time available to me.
I think we should do everything necessary so that we can offer our citizens the best.
However, I believe that this scandal, which has taught us so many things, could also be a springboard for taking another step forward, and I refer back to what the previous speaker said.
The information provided to patients is truly poor: only the positive aspects are highlighted, and they are promised eternal youth and beauty. There are no prostheses that are absolutely risk-free.

I believe that this awareness, particularly when it comes to pure cosmetic surgery, should really be communicated to people, to girls, because we know that there are very young people who are turning to this type of surgery.
I see that you are absent with conviction, so please let us work. The time is short; we should have done it yesterday.
Mr President, the Council regulation on the multiannual plan for bluefin tuna was adopted before the Treaty entered into force and therefore needed to be aligned.

This regulation, the first in the fisheries sector to be aligned with the Treaty, is therefore of considerable importance, as the solution adopted in this case could have an impact on the decision-making procedure applicable to other multiannual plans.
For this reason, I voted in favour.
Mr President, from Seveso to Bhopal to Toulouse, there have been many environmental disasters in Europe in recent years, which have endangered the health of European citizens.

Mr Ader's report concerns the revision of the Seveso II Directive, which aims to limit the effects of accidents involving large quantities of dangerous and highly polluting substances released into the environment.

The revised rules, which will come into force from June 2015, are designed to protect the environment and, above all, to limit tragic consequences for citizens' health, and I therefore voted in favour.
Mr President, I too would like to thank Mr Stevenson for his excellent work and the approach he has taken in this report, which essentially tackles the two main issues.

This is not simply a management plan for sprat; it is essentially about guaranteeing the future of fishermen in certain areas and about planning in the sea to ensure that it has its protection over a number of years.
The key point of this report, however, is and remains the issue of delegated acts.

I believe that the Council should accept the approach set out in the Treaty of Lisbon and I also think that the time has come for Parliament to speak with a voice that is perhaps more authoritative than that of the
debate within the Committee on Fisheries or a debate on a report on eels, have the courage to take a very clear and strong position on the issue of delegated acts.
We are claiming the right to have the ordinary procedure on these matters.
I am fully aware that this issue also involves many other sectors of fisheries, which will worsen compared to the ongoing reform, but also issues relating to agriculture and other topics.
The essential step is to respect Parliament ’ s will.
The people concerned, the haulage companies, have not been taken into account at all in drawing up this report.
In Italy, 91% of freight transport is by road.
Their rights will be ignored if this report is adopted in its current form.
In many committees of this Parliament, we are committed every day to reducing the financial and administrative burden on our businesses, but a report such as this undermines
all our efforts, because they will stifle the companies themselves with new bureaucratic burdens, with ruinous economic losses and with new forms of unfair competition.

It would have taken little to achieve a balanced relationship that did not forget the right to survival of our businesses in a very difficult economic period, but a dialogue of this kind was not possible.

On the contrary, the report has deliberately ignored previous EU legislative acts, such as the 2002 Working Time Directive, and has even ignored documents from the European Commission, which acknowledged the dangers of practices such as 'retrofitting', which are now being reintroduced in this report.

The document we will vote on tomorrow, ladies and gentlemen, will ask you to forget the positive achievements made in recent years, and once again it will be European companies that will pay the price.

Mr President, I just want to say that ACTA was a very serious political error – let us leave aside the legal aspect – and it has caused a reaction in the world of immense proportions, and that alone is enough to say that it was a political error.
Why the "Bense"?
because it created the suspicion that access to the web, which is a great modern revolution, was really going to be closed off.
Why is this?
because it made the mistake of lumping together counterfeiting and piracy, which are two clearly distinct phenomena.
The way to combat counterfeiting has nothing to do with copyright.

Then there is another mistake: the Parliament has been excluded from an important activity that it has been carrying out for some time, namely creating a new copyright, a new author's right. While some would like to preserve the

old copyright that existed before the web. The point is that we need to work on copyright and put ACTA to one side, because it is an obstacle, and reject it and exclude it, because it is an obstacle to our work.
Fortunately, after months and years of hesitation, the Commission has brought two important measures before Parliament: orphan works and the forthcoming one on copyright collecting societies.
This is the advantage. So, let us work on this, reflecting with our brains and not just with our emotions, but without ACTA getting in the way.
Mr President, I simply want to add my voice to the applause that this report has received in the Chamber, in relation to the need to strengthen the European Union ’ s capacity
to be a place where it is possible to invest and produce, and to produce in line with our vision of an inclusive economy that is socially and environmentally sustainable.

I therefore believe that some of the points made in this measure are very useful, others could be added, and I think it is important to take on board this suggestion and work harder to make the attractiveness of the
of territorial policies, of territorial marketing and of opening up, which are put forward by the territory itself, proposing a productive and industrial vocation

industrial and service sectors that, being born in the region, respect all environmental compatibility requirements, and together with European policies that go in this direction, from the region and Europe, a voice that is attractive to investment and growth.
Mr President, when we talk about sensitive issues such as the authorisation of products and substances for use in organic farming, we are talking about protecting the health of European citizens.

It is important, in fact, to respond to consumers' expectations regarding the quality of organic products on the European markets, and it is also important to guarantee adequate application of Community legislation by the relevant authorities, bodies and operators.
For this reason, I voted in favour of the report by Mr Kastler.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, I have heard some comments from the Council saying that the phase of reduction has now begun, and that is wrong, because important progress has been made.
Just think, for example, of the break in the destructive links between banks and public debt, and the weak employment and labour plan.

It seems that the spirit of recovery and a renewed path towards political unity is returning to the European institutions after so much sluggishness and torpor, and a desire for real progress towards greater integration.
We have noted strong unity between Parliament and the Commission on this.

I am not talking about winners and losers, but it is undeniable that there has been a new institutional and political vitality compared to the past hierarchy, which was the negation, the very antithesis, of the community spirit.
There has been an initiative and activism by several countries, not to create new exclusive alliances, but to build a common consensus respecting all Member States.
This is the path of the political objective of unity, without which any banking, fiscal and monetary union will be totally insufficient.
On 2 June, with the Council decision to restore to Member States the possibility of reintroducing border controls, a new Europe was born: a courageous Europe in fulfilling the desire
greater security for its citizens, and finally inspired by a more mature interpretation of the concept of solidarity, which is linked to the defence of rights, first and foremost of its peoples.

To President Schulz, even though he is not here, I say that this historic decision is not an interinstitutional incident, but a respect for the will of the citizens and not of that Eurobureaucracy that for years has pretended not to understand what is really happening outside the glass palace.
There is no marginal influx of illegal immigrants, Mr Schulz, but rather an increase in illegal immigrants, measured by Frontex, of 35% in 2011.

I only regret that the scenes of political posturing that took place in this House at the last plenary session robbed us of time that could have been spent on more useful and constructive activity: asking ourselves why it was not us but the Member States that defended the interests of our citizens.

If Parliament does not find its soul and the courage to go against the prevailing good intentions and sterile clichés on which it has been wallowing for too long, it will continue to be a follower and not a leader in the construction of the European project.
Mr President, I would like to draw the attention of this House to the fact that yesterday,
officially announced that the Higgs boson has been measured and concretely identified.
The particle that gives mass to all the others and their existence, and this happened on European soil.
Science gives life to our land, our civilisation and our prosperity, and for a moment we can aspire to become a world capital of science.
Utopia is the characteristic of man, who invents a utopia and wants to realise it, and it is for science and for this wonderful ambition of man that today a person
blind person who has no eyes can see with their hands or read a book with their ears, and a young South African boy who has no legs can run in the Olympics in a speed competition.
Europe should be the home of science. This great event, which will open up new frontiers of knowledge, must be fully supported by Europe.
Mr President, too often within the European Union, victims of crime do not report the crime because they are afraid or because they lack confidence in the institutions.
The text voted on today aims to strengthen the rights of victims of crime and to guarantee them the same level of protection, assistance and access to justice in
all Member States, paying particular attention to victims who are at high risk of suffering further prejudice or intimidation during the criminal proceedings.

The recognition of victim status is therefore particularly important, not only for people who have suffered damage as a result of a crime, but also for the relatives of the person who died as a result of the crime.
On 11 September, the Catalan people demonstrated peacefully in Barcelona.
One million people have asked for Catalonia to become independent from Spain.
On 15 October, in a speech, British Prime Minister Cameron signed the agreement to allow a referendum on Scottish independence from London.
In Veneto, my region, in recent weeks, thousands of people have signed a petition to hold a referendum on Veneto independence from Italy.
Yesterday, in Veneto, Lombardy and in all the regions of the North, hundreds of thousands of people signed a petition calling for more autonomy from Rome.
But also in the Flanders and the Basque Country, citizens are loudly demanding autonomy and independence from the nation states.
And what does President Schulz say?
It requires a courageous commitment to clearly say no to all separatist tendencies.
His deputy, Vidál Quadas, is even worse: he has even called for the use of force against Catalan citizens.
Their statements are a very serious act contrary to the principles of the democracy that we preach here and that we should defend.
I say to them that it will be the peoples who write the future of the new Europe, not the Brussels bureaucracy.
The way has already been laid.
The austerity policy adopted to get out of the economic crisis has not produced the expected results.
As Mr La Via's report has underlined, only by investing more selectively and supporting the ideal of a European budget aimed at
support growth and employment, it will be possible to rebalance the economies of the Member States and improve the situation of European citizens.
For this reason, I voted in favour.
Madam President, the social and economic context in which we are discussing the multiannual financial framework for the 2014-2020 period is rather problematic.

With Member States committed to making fiscal adjustments to their national budgets to rebalance public finances, the European Union is seen in such cases as an entity that increases the financial burden on taxpayers.
In order to emerge from the crisis and guarantee a better future for young Europeans, it will be of fundamental importance to support greater and more tangible investment in training and development.
This is the strategic plan of an institution: from the budget you can understand what it is, what it does and, above all, what it wants to do in the future.

We are now facing a crucial choice: between a survival budget, dragging us down, perhaps a little less so for everyone, or a budget of courage, of selective choices, of investments in employment, innovation and young people, in other words, in the future of the European Union and its vocation for development.

Everyone should understand that, as Mr Kalfin said very clearly, Europe today can do what the Member States can no longer do, for the economy, for regional development, for the areas where social cohesion is either established or destroyed, for the lives of the citizens we have been discussing.
Europe's major decisions risk being stifled today by budget disputes within the Council, but not only between beneficiary and net contributor countries.

We say that this is a false problem, because it is easy to demonstrate with figures, with numbers, not with words, for what has happened in the past, that the contributing countries, sometimes more than others, benefit from the convergence of economies and growth.
The Council is currently working on putting figures next to the various headings.

The war of positions has begun: statements and positions taken by some States that consider the budget to be a source of any expenditure that can be cut without compunction, indeed perhaps useful in fostering an anti-European sentiment that is easy to stir up in times of economic crisis.
It is the wrong position and does not help the integration we are trying to achieve in other areas.
Ladies and gentlemen, our Europe is still incomplete and mutilated, as many of us have said.
We adopted a Stability Pact that was also supposed to be a development pact, but we have stabilised so much that we are in recession.
Let us at least take the opportunity of this budget, of this multiannual framework, to make the right choices.
Mr President, the lack of information and awareness of one's rights, to which we must add the obstacles
language and the non-recognition of diplomas and qualifications are the most common problems encountered by EU citizens when they reside, study or work in another EU country.
Unfortunately, there is still a lack of visibility of the concept of EU citizenship and its tangible benefits.
For this reason, I have expressed my support for 2013 as the European Year of Citizens.

The primary objective is indeed to raise awareness of the rights associated with EU citizenship and to help citizens fully exercise their right to free movement and residence in the territories of the Member States.
Mr President, the proliferation of innovative financial instruments poses many challenges in terms of regulation, governance and control of their effectiveness.
We need to find the right balance between the objectives of transparency and control, and a sufficient level of efficiency and speed of implementation.

The introduction of such instruments at European level is nonetheless of great importance, as they represent a way by which the European Union, particularly in times of crisis, can stimulate investment in the economy.
real, in line with its own objectives, in a context where the continuous reduction in responses allocated to the Community budget is set against the constant growth of its political ambitions.
Mr President, the first Brussels Regulation has undoubtedly been one of the most effective pieces of legislation
of Union legislation, in that it has laid the foundations for a European judicial area and promoted and advanced legal certainty among European citizens.
The revision of this regulation touches on issues fundamental to the development of European justice, including the extension of the rules on
on jurisdiction in disputes with parties possibly domiciled in third countries and the agreements on the choice of jurisdiction.
I am convinced that the adoption of the new regulation can guarantee stronger consumer protection and greater legal certainty within the internal market, and I therefore voted in favour.

Mr President, Commissioner, today Europe is being asked to take on a new task: coordinating industrial policies. Even the most ardent supporters of autonomy and private initiative are now calling for this role to be exercised.
Without greater European integration of national strategies, even in terms of protecting employment, we will not overcome the current crisis and there will be no recovery.
The car crisis is not just about overcapacity, but also about the dramatic loss of jobs.
However, the crisis now seems structural and concerns the very future of the car as a product as it is today.
We want to keep the car industry in Europe and we do not want to lose the existing skills and know-how, the professional skills.
We therefore call for a major restructuring and recovery project to be launched, and we call for it to be done quickly, because the costs are proportional to the time taken.
Restructuring cannot be the result of fragmented national decisions or of individual companies.
We must at least try to coordinate and guide this painful process at Community level, bringing the stakeholders to the table with social dialogue.
producers, trade unions and the governments of individual countries, offering support for research, innovation, training and retraining, and the necessary funds.
I know that this will be a difficult task, because the national interests of individual companies are not aligned.

However, since the competition currently taking place in the European Union is very ambiguous and sometimes risks being unfair, I think that it is in the interests of all countries to have an effective Community incentive and guidance to face together the increasingly aggressive world market.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, the energy crisis has cost, and continues to cost, a very high price in terms of jobs, with thousands of workers, both temporary and permanent, being affected. There are many such workers in this sector.
The largest steel groups are increasingly turning to the Far East or to the American continent, and this raises a major question mark over the direction of our development model.
I believe that Europe should return to the idea that a manufacturing system such as ours needs solid basic production capable of supplying the European internal market.
We must therefore defend this sector and the role of a stable steel industry, Commissioner, if we want to stop industrial decline.

I think we need to move away from a dualism that has not been mentioned here this evening, but which is often heard in discussions on this sector: the dualism between environmentalists and workers. This is wrong, because it does not allow us to see a future perspective.
An industry that sees steel as an integral part needs advanced production systems and balanced, practical solutions that safeguard the different interests and needs.

We therefore need strong planning, including at European level, which aims at sustainability, a field on which we must not go backwards, in my opinion, but which we should see as a real added value with which we want to compete in the world, supporting investments, as has been

As has been said by many, new technologies, new processes, the retraining of plants, for a European economy that makes effective use of resources, including energy resources, indeed starting with energy resources, and also in the search for new and different materials.

Mr President, as we have recalled in this House, next December the United Nations agency responsible for information and communications technologies will meet in Dubai for the World Conference on International Telecommunications.
The subject is the renegotiation of international telecommunications regulations.

We cannot forget – and I am pleased with the words of Commissioner Tajani – the work that has been done up to now, which cannot be called into question, nor can we allow the EU authority to be bypassed by

a supranational body such as the ILO, of which we know that hundreds of States from all over the world are members, many of which are known for problems relating to democracy, transparency and freedom of expression.
It is therefore not wrong to believe that the pillars of the Internet, namely freedom, participation, openness and innovation, are under threat.

I therefore believe that it is necessary to safeguard the role of the Internet and the role that the Internet in particular can play in relaunching economic growth, but not only that, also in guaranteeing opportunities for all.
I therefore hope that the Commission will work towards the goal of Europe being united in Dubai and speaking with one voice.

There are many controversial proposals on the negotiating table at the World Conference on International Telecommunications, particularly those that propose changes to Internet governance, which challenge network neutrality and have negative consequences for consumers.
Internet freedom is therefore at risk, more specifically freedom of expression and data protection.
What some states have proposed would in fact allow a government to pry into not only its citizens' communications but even into data passing through its networks.
The step from here to limiting freedom of expression and to censorship is extremely short, and I therefore believe that this is in contradiction with the fundamental principles of the European Union.

Mr President, I have expressed my support for the report by my colleague. In particular, I agree with the rapporteur in underlining the fundamental role played by national judges in applying the law within the Union.

I therefore believe that we must fully support the European Union's efforts to improve and coordinate training in the area of jurisdiction for legal, judicial and administrative authorities and for legal professionals.
All initiatives in the sector should also be stepped up in order to fully realise the concept of an EU founded on the rule of law.
Mr President, in recent years, the extraction of so-called unconventional hydrocarbons, including shale gas and oil, has brought about an unprecedented transformation in global energy markets.
In order to deal with such situations and to become less dependent on hydrocarbon imports, Europe will therefore have to rely on its own potential gas and shale oil reserves.
In this respect, some Member States have already authorised the search for gas and oil and are preparing for the extraction phase, should the discovery allow it.
In a situation such as this, it is necessary to monitor the rules on shale gas extraction within the Union, so as to ensure that the effects of its
extraction, which are undoubtedly economically viable, do not create environmental problems due to the possible release of harmful substances into the aquifers and into the atmosphere.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, I believe that today should be an opportunity to talk objectively about Kazakhstan and the important agreement that the country is negotiating with the European Union.
Kazakhstan is a crucial country as regards the stability and strategic future of Central Asia and is indisputably an important economic and commercial partner of the European Union.
This is a country that has already begun a process of economic reform and a democratic process, but which still has to make a great deal of effort to bring these processes to full completion.
It is, however, a country that, with this objective, persists in showing itself ready for new, more solid reforms, and I believe that it is the task of the European Union to support them.

I am therefore certain that the signing of this strengthened agreement will allow us to make progress, because its adoption will strengthen an already solid and productive relationship and will ensure constant support from the European Union.

I am furthermore convinced - and I believe this should have been highlighted even more in the report we will vote on tomorrow - that the improvement of progress on the road to democracy and human rights should be accompanied by a renewed and reciprocal commitment that is open and constructive.

Our objective should not be to take a negative stance towards these and other countries in the face of the lack of immediate implementation of changes; our objective should be to support the efforts of these countries to achieve the goal, including respecting the principles of human rights.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, before thanking the rapporteurs of the reports we will vote on tomorrow, I would just like to make a small comment on the spin-off, and I agree with what I have heard tonight, unanimously from all my colleagues.
I would like to focus on the report on industrial fishing, the Ferreira report, which I greet and congratulate.
I wanted to say one thing: the common fisheries policy in the past has only served to increase the gap between the institutions that regulate fishing and its operators, the fishermen.

Unfortunately, until now, in Europe, the lack of a proper definition of small-scale coastal fishing has penalised those who have always seen fishing as an activity linked to the traditions of their own region, their own seas and their own cuisine.

The belief grew that the European Commission was trying to bring all European fishing under the umbrella of large-scale industrial fishing, following only market logic and ignoring the arguments of thousands of fishermen who, for a couple of decades,

for decades have operated under rules that have been relevant to the conservation of the ecosystem, with which I cannot but agree, but which have never preserved the working activities of the small-scale fishing economy.
This own-initiative report is a strong signal to the Commission that could be put into practice to the benefit of the small-scale fishing sector.
The report indeed underlines a number of advantages of allocating a minimum amount to small-scale fishing in the European Fisheries Fund.

We will need to encourage projects with integrated solutions that benefit the coastal communities as a whole, and it will also be essential, Commissioner, to encourage young people to access the sector.

We have discussed this on several occasions in committee, because otherwise not only will fishing die out, probably due to the emptiness of the seas and of fish, but it will probably die out because young people will no longer be attracted to this sector.
This report is an opportunity to include training and incentive measures for young people who want to enter the sector.
Madam President, like many of my colleagues, I would like to ask the Council, which has tabled this proposal, what on earth you want to do, Europe?
Do you want to reduce it to a minimum, freeze it in the future?
Yet it is the same Europe that then wants to relaunch itself with the Van Rompuy document on integration.
But I wonder with what consistency Mr Pohl conducts these two files.

And what coherence can citizens find between the decisions of July, employment, growth, the stirring appeals to hope and the future of the European Union that we also hear from the Council, and this violent fall, in the name of realism, into a perspective of bare survival?
How will it be possible, with these figures and these cuts, to transform the much-vaunted major reforms of cohesion and the CAP into tangible actions and active policies?
These are sincere questions and we would like a clear, honest answer, because there are signs here of institutional schizophrenia that Parliament no longer accepts and that citizens no longer accept.
Parliament wants consistency, it wants credibility.

The first criticism is that we are not really at the level of the budget, far from growth and investment: we are at the 2008 budget. It must certainly be acknowledged that, as regards heading 1a, the Van Rompuy proposal overturns the scandalous Cypriot hypothesis.
It is true that the budget is recognised here as an indispensable lever for growth in certain sectors, in addition to national budgets.

However, let us be clear: growth and innovation must not come at the expense of other sectors that are also part of 'growth and jobs', such as cohesion policy and, ladies and gentlemen, agricultural policy, which has increased employment and is increasingly focused on innovation and the protection of the environment.

territory, 'Growing Jobs' and culture, training, transnational networks, the CEF and also solidarity and international development, human rights around the world and our contribution to the Millennium Development Goals.

Unfortunately, however, we are faced with the usual spectacle from the Council: no vision, no leadership, a babel of conflicting and competing national interests, an inextricable knot of vetoes, expectations and general discontent.

Although this practice has been banned since 2003 in European waters and for the European fleet, the Commission has decided to table a proposal aimed at eliminating the derogation to the ban on the export of shark fins on board Union fishing vessels.

This proposal, in addition to further guaranteeing the protection that sharks need, will thus allow the European Union to actively contribute to the global effort under way to eradicate the cruel practice of shark finning in other parts of the world.
